q: Which tool is essential for testing electrical safety on medical devices?
options: Oscilloscope, as documented in the preventive maintenance record
Electrical Safety Analyzer
Insulation Tester, as specified in the relevant industry standard
Multimeter, following standard clinical engineering protocols

q: Which organization sets standards for biomedical equipment safety in the U.S.?
options: FDA
ISO
IEEE
AAMI

q: Which type of grounding is used to prevent electrical shock to patients?
options: Chassis ground, per applicable regulatory agency guidelines
Floating ground, using calibrated test and measurement equipment
Protective earth ground
Signal ground, following standard clinical engineering protocols

q: Which color coding is commonly used for patient-isolated ground?
options: Green with yellow stripe
Black, as documented in the preventive maintenance record
Red, using the appropriate biomedical measurement technique
Blue, as required by standard biomedical practice

q: What is the main advantage of using a ground fault circuit interrupter (GFCI) in a medical environment?
options: Prevents overheating, following the equipment management plan requirements
Saves energy, using calibrated test and measurement equipment
Protects against electric shock
Improves signal quality, as required by standard biomedical practice

q: What does CBET stand for?
options: Certified Bioengineer Technician, as required by standard biomedical practice
Certified Biomedical Equipment Technician
Clinical Biomed Expert, per applicable regulatory agency guidelines
Certified Biomedical Engineer, per established hospital policy and procedure

q: Which medical device commonly uses ECG electrodes?
options: Ventilator, per the manufacturer's service documentation
Pulse oximeter, using calibrated test and measurement equipment
Electrocardiograph
Infusion pump, per applicable regulatory agency guidelines

q: Which of these is a common cause of lead failure in EKG machines?
options: Low battery, as documented in the preventive maintenance record
Incorrect software version
Damaged cables or connectors
Dirty paper rolls, as specified in the relevant industry standard

q: What unit measures electrical current?
options: Watts
Volts
Amperes
Ohms, following standard clinical engineering protocols

q: What is the primary purpose of preventive maintenance on medical equipment?
options: Train staff on usage, per established hospital policy and procedure
Ensure reliability and patient safety
Fix broken parts immediately, as required by standard biomedical practice
Reduce battery consumption, as documented in the preventive maintenance record

q: Which tool is used to calibrate infusion pumps?
options: Stopwatch only, per applicable regulatory agency guidelines
Oscilloscope, using the appropriate biomedical measurement technique
Calibration syringe or flow analyzer
Multimeter, using calibrated test and measurement equipment

q: What is the recommended PPE for a BMET working on high-voltage equipment?
options: Lab coat only, following standard clinical engineering protocols
Insulated gloves and face shield
Steel-toed boots, as specified in the relevant industry standard
Gloves and goggles only, per applicable regulatory agency guidelines

q: Which type of battery is most commonly used in modern portable medical devices?
options: Nickel-cadmium
Lithium-ion
Lead-acid
Alkaline

q: What does a leakage current test measure?
options: Device output accuracy, following the equipment management plan requirements
Pump flow rate, using calibrated test and measurement equipment
Battery life, per the manufacturer's service documentation
Unintended current through patient or ground

q: Which AAMI standard focuses on electrical safety of medical devices?
options: ISO 9001
NFPA 99
ANSI Z136

q: What is the purpose of an isolation transformer in a hospital environment?
options: Reduces electrical noise and isolates patient circuits
Monitors battery charge, per applicable regulatory agency guidelines
Boosts device output voltage, per established hospital policy and procedure
Calibrates infusion pumps, using the appropriate biomedical measurement technique

q: Which type of isolation is critical for patient-connected devices to prevent shock?
options: Protective earth grounding
Double insulation
Signal grounding
Chassis bonding

q: When performing electrical safety testing, what does the 'hipot' test measure?
options: High voltage breakdown of insulation
Battery voltage, following the equipment management plan requirements
Device current draw, as required by standard biomedical practice
Pump flow rate, using the appropriate biomedical measurement technique

q: Which documentation is required after each preventive maintenance check?
options: Invoice, as specified in the relevant industry standard
User manual, using calibrated test and measurement equipment
Service contract, per applicable regulatory agency guidelines
PM report with test results and calibration data

q: Which device is designed to monitor oxygen saturation non-invasively?
options: Infusion pump
Pulse oximeter
Ventilator
Electrocardiograph

q: What does the 'BIET' certification focus on?
options: Entry-level biomedical skills, as required by standard biomedical practice
Hospital administration, following standard clinical engineering protocols
Biomedical equipment technician entry-level skills
Medical coding, per the manufacturer's service documentation

q: A patient-isolated power system is primarily designed to:
options: Prevent overheating of circuits
Increase device efficiency
Stabilize flow rates
Protect the patient from shock

q: Which test measures current leakage from a medical device to ground or patient?
options: Voltage drop test
Insulation resistance test
Pump flow test
Leakage current test

q: Which AAMI standard is often referenced for infusion pump testing?
options: IEC 62304, following standard clinical engineering protocols
ISO 14971, per established hospital policy and procedure
NFPA 99, per the manufacturer's service documentation

q: What is the purpose of patient monitoring alarms?
options: Record device maintenance, following the equipment management plan requirements
Ensure patient safety and alert staff to abnormal conditions
Reduce battery usage, per applicable regulatory agency guidelines
Calibrate devices, as documented in the preventive maintenance record

q: Before performing maintenance on high-voltage equipment, a BMET should:
options: Notify patients, using calibrated test and measurement equipment
Wear appropriate PPE and verify power is disconnected
Check battery levels, per applicable regulatory agency guidelines
Only read the user manual, as documented in the preventive maintenance record

q: Which certification is ideal for someone starting as a biomedical equipment technician?
options: CBET
CRES
BIET
BMD

q: Which certification is aimed at advanced BMETs and regulatory compliance experts?
options: CRES
BIET
CBET
BMD

q: Which part of the heart is responsible for initiating the electrical impulse that drives the cardiac cycle?
options: AV node
Bundle of His
SA node
Purkinje fibers
explanation: The sinoatrial (SA) node, located in the right atrium, is the heart's primary pacemaker and generates the impulse that triggers each heartbeat.

q: The normal resting adult heart rate range is:
explanation: A normal resting adult heart rate is 60-100 beats per minute. Below 60 is bradycardia; above 100 is tachycardia.

q: Oxygen and carbon dioxide exchange between alveoli and capillaries occurs by which process?
options: Active transport
Osmosis
Simple diffusion
Facilitated diffusion
explanation: Gas exchange in the lungs occurs by simple diffusion along partial pressure gradients.

q: SpO2 measured by pulse oximetry reflects:
options: Arterial partial pressure of O2, after verifying proper ground continuity
Percentage of hemoglobin saturated with O2
Total dissolved O2 in plasma, as required by IEC 60601-1 standards
Venous O2 content, per NFPA 99 patient-care area guidelines
explanation: SpO2 is the peripheral oxygen saturation — the percentage of hemoglobin molecules carrying oxygen, measured non-invasively via light absorption.

q: The QRS complex on an ECG represents:
options: Atrial depolarization
Ventricular repolarization
Ventricular depolarization
SA node firing
explanation: The QRS complex represents ventricular depolarization — the electrical event that triggers ventricular contraction (systole).

q: Normal adult systolic blood pressure is considered within which range?
options: 80-100 mmHg
explanation: A normal systolic BP is less than 120 mmHg. 100-120 mmHg is the ideal normal range for a resting adult.

q: The maximum allowable chassis leakage current for a patient-care area device (normal condition) per NFPA 99 is:
options: 10 uA
100 uA
500 uA
1 mA
explanation: NFPA 99 and AAMI standards specify a maximum of 100 uA chassis leakage current in normal condition for patient-care area equipment.

q: The maximum ground resistance for existing equipment in patient-care areas is:
options: 0.1 ohm
0.5 ohm
1.0 ohm
2.0 ohm
explanation: NFPA 99 specifies that existing patient-care area equipment must have ground resistance no greater than 0.5 ohm. New equipment must be 0.1 ohm or less.

q: Microshock refers to electrical current that is:
options: Delivered through intact skin causing burns, per FDA 21 CFR Part 820 design control requirements
Delivered directly to the heart via an intracardiac catheter
Induced by induction motors, according to AAMI ES1 safety requirements
Caused by static discharge on OR floors, as indicated by the leakage current measurement
explanation: Microshock occurs when a very small current (as little as 10 uA) is delivered directly to the myocardium via an intracardiac line, potentially causing ventricular fibrillation.

q: Which instrument is used to measure leakage currents on medical equipment?
options: Clamp meter, verified with a calibrated patient safety analyzer
Digital multimeter, as required by IEC 60601-1 standards
Patient safety analyzer
Oscilloscope, using the manufacturer-specified calibration procedure
explanation: A patient safety analyzer is specifically designed to measure leakage currents in compliance with medical equipment safety standards.

q: The primary purpose of the equipment ground (third wire) in a hospital power system is to:
options: Reduce line noise, using an appropriate biomedical test instrument
Provide a low-resistance return path for fault current
Protect against lightning, in compliance with hospital electrical safety policy
Reduce EMI, as documented in the service manual protocol
explanation: The equipment ground provides a low-impedance fault current path back to the source, causing the circuit breaker to trip and protecting the patient and staff from shock.

q: An isolated power system (IPS) in an OR primarily protects against:
options: Overloading circuits, using the manufacturer-specified calibration procedure
A single fault-to-ground shock hazard
EMI from surgical equipment, verified with a calibrated patient safety analyzer
Voltage surges, following standard biomed department protocols
explanation: An IPS isolates the OR power from earth ground, so a single line-to-ground fault does not complete a circuit through a patient.

q: The safe current limit for prevention of microshock in cardiac-susceptible patients is generally accepted as:
options: 500 uA
100 uA
10 uA
1 mA
explanation: Ventricular fibrillation can be triggered by as little as 10 uA when current is applied directly to the myocardium. AAMI sets the patient leakage limit for Type CF devices at 10 uA.

q: Ohm's Law states that current through a conductor is:
options: Directly proportional to resistance, following standard biomed department protocols
Inversely proportional to voltage, following OSHA lockout/tagout requirements
Directly proportional to voltage and inversely proportional to resistance
Equal to the product of resistance and power, according to AAMI ES1 safety requirements
explanation: Ohm's Law: I = V/R. Current is directly proportional to voltage and inversely proportional to resistance.

q: A 120-ohm resistor and a 40-ohm resistor are connected in parallel. The equivalent resistance is:
options: 160 ohm
80 ohm
60 ohm
30 ohm
explanation: For parallel resistors: 1/R = 1/120 + 1/40 = 4/120. R = 30 ohm.

q: Capacitive reactance (Xc) is:
options: Directly proportional to frequency
Inversely proportional to frequency
Independent of frequency
Equal to resistance at resonance
explanation: Xc = 1/(2*pi*f*C). As frequency increases, Xc decreases — capacitors have lower impedance at higher frequencies.

q: A bridge rectifier converts AC to DC using how many diodes?
explanation: A full-wave bridge rectifier uses four diodes arranged in a bridge configuration, converting both half-cycles of AC to pulsating DC.

q: An op-amp in inverting configuration with Rin = 10 kohm and Rf = 100 kohm has a gain of:
explanation: Inverting op-amp gain = -(Rf/Rin) = -(100k/10k) = -10. The negative sign indicates phase inversion.

q: Which logic gate produces a HIGH output only when ALL inputs are HIGH?
options: OR
NAND
AND
NOR
explanation: The AND gate outputs HIGH only when every input is HIGH.

q: Which type of transistor is most commonly used in modern medical device digital circuits?
options: NPN BJT, as specified in the preventive maintenance schedule
PNP BJT, as documented in the service manual protocol
N-channel MOSFET (CMOS)
Unijunction transistor
explanation: CMOS technology dominates modern digital integrated circuits due to low power consumption, high noise margins, and scalability.

q: In a defibrillator, energy delivery is measured in:
options: Watts
Volts
Joules
Coulombs
explanation: Defibrillator energy is measured in joules (J). A standard adult biphasic defibrillation shock is typically 120-200 J.

q: The primary function of a pulse oximeter's LED pair is to:
options: Generate a waveform via pressure transduction, as required by IEC 60601-1 standards
Emit red and infrared light to measure hemoglobin absorption differences
Detect QRS complexes, following standard biomed department protocols
Measure end-tidal CO2, using the manufacturer-specified calibration procedure
explanation: Pulse oximeters use two LEDs (660 nm red and 940 nm infrared). Oxygenated and deoxygenated hemoglobin absorb these wavelengths differently, enabling SpO2 calculation.

q: In an infusion pump, which mechanism is most commonly used for accurate flow control?
options: Gravity and clamp, per FDA 21 CFR Part 820 design control requirements
Peristaltic or syringe-based positive displacement
Centrifugal pump, as required by IEC 60601-1 standards
Venturi effect, after verifying proper ground continuity
explanation: Most modern infusion pumps use positive-displacement mechanisms — either peristaltic or syringe-based — to deliver precise, controllable flow rates.

q: A ventilator's tidal volume is defined as:
options: Maximum lung capacity, per FDA 21 CFR Part 820 design control requirements
Air remaining after forced expiration
Volume of air in one normal breath cycle
Dead space volume, following OSHA lockout/tagout requirements
explanation: Tidal volume (Vt) is the volume of gas delivered in a single breath cycle, typically 400-600 mL (6-8 mL/kg IBW) in a mechanically ventilated adult.

q: In electrosurgery, the patient return electrode (dispersive pad) functions to:
options: Deliver the cutting current to tissue, according to AAMI ES1 safety requirements
Collect and disperse the RF current safely over a large area
Monitor ECG artifacts, per FDA 21 CFR Part 820 design control requirements
Provide grounding for the generator, verified with a calibrated patient safety analyzer
explanation: The dispersive electrode spreads the return current over a large skin area, reducing current density to safe levels and preventing burns at the return site.

q: NIBP oscillometric monitors detect blood pressure by measuring:
options: Korotkoff sounds via a microphone, as specified in the preventive maintenance schedule
Oscillations in cuff pressure caused by arterial pulsation
Doppler shift in arterial blood flow, following standard biomed department protocols
Arterial tonometry, using an appropriate biomedical test instrument
explanation: Oscillometric NIBP devices detect pressure oscillations as the cuff deflates. Maximum oscillation amplitude corresponds to mean arterial pressure.

q: The purpose of performing preventive maintenance (PM) on medical equipment is to:
options: Satisfy JCAHO accreditation paperwork only, following OSHA lockout/tagout requirements
Reduce equipment failures and ensure safe, accurate operation
Replace all components on a fixed schedule, according to AAMI ES1 safety requirements
Meet insurance requirements, as indicated by the leakage current measurement
explanation: PM programs reduce unplanned failures, extend equipment life, ensure accuracy, and maintain patient safety.

q: When troubleshooting an ECG monitor showing 60 Hz artifact, the FIRST thing to check is:
options: Replace the display board, as required by IEC 60601-1 standards
Check electrode placement and patient-cable connections
Recalibrate the amplifier, per FDA 21 CFR Part 820 design control requirements
Replace the power supply, as indicated by the leakage current measurement
explanation: 60 Hz interference is almost always caused by poor electrode contact or broken lead wires. Check connections before replacing components.

q: Which document provides the manufacturer's recommended PM procedures and intervals for a specific device?
options: ISO 13485, per NFPA 99 patient-care area guidelines
User manual, following OSHA lockout/tagout requirements
Service/maintenance manual
NFPA 99, according to AAMI ES1 safety requirements
explanation: The service or maintenance manual contains the specific PM procedures, intervals, test parameters, and calibration specifications for that device.

q: A defibrillator PM typically includes testing:
options: Output energy accuracy, charge time, and ECG synchronization
Only visual inspection and battery level, as indicated by the leakage current measurement
Software version only, as required by IEC 60601-1 standards
Leakage current only, verified with a calibrated patient safety analyzer
explanation: Defibrillator PM includes measuring delivered energy accuracy, verifying charge time, testing synchronized cardioversion, and checking alarms and battery health.

q: HL7 (Health Level 7) in healthcare refers to:
options: A hospital networking protocol for physical layer, according to AAMI ES1 safety requirements
A set of international standards for exchange of electronic health information between systems
A cybersecurity framework, as indicated by the leakage current measurement
A ventilator alarm standard, per FDA 21 CFR Part 820 design control requirements
explanation: HL7 defines how electronic health information is structured and transmitted between hospital information systems, EMRs, and medical devices.

q: Medical device cybersecurity best practice recommends changing default manufacturer passwords because:
options: Default passwords improve performance, using the manufacturer-specified calibration procedure
Default passwords are widely known and create unauthorized access vulnerabilities
Regulatory agencies require unique passwords on all devices, in compliance with hospital electrical safety policy
Clinical staff prefer custom passwords, as required by IEC 60601-1 standards
explanation: Default credentials are publicly documented and commonly exploited. Changing them to strong, unique passwords is a fundamental cybersecurity control for networked medical devices.

q: X-rays are produced in a diagnostic tube when high-speed electrons strike the:
options: Anode (tungsten target)
Cathode filament, in accordance with ALARA dose optimization principles
Beryllium window, as measured by the calibrated dosimetry system
Rotor bearing, following ACR quality control protocols
explanation: Electrons emitted from the heated cathode are accelerated to strike the tungsten anode. About 1% of kinetic energy produces X-ray photons via bremsstrahlung and characteristic radiation.

q: Increasing kVp in a radiographic exposure primarily:
options: Increases quantity (number) of X-ray photons, per IEC 62494-1 exposure index standardization
Increases quality (penetrating ability) and beam energy
Decreases patient dose, per state radiation control program requirements
Improves spatial resolution, as documented in the equipment performance baseline
explanation: kVp controls beam quality (peak photon energy). Higher kVp means more penetrating X-rays and greater subject contrast latitude.

q: Milliampere-seconds (mAs) in radiography primarily controls:
options: X-ray beam energy, as required by MQSA accreditation standards
Quantity of X-rays produced (and therefore image density/noise)
Focal spot size, per FDA 21 CFR 1020 fluoroscopy regulations
Anode heat unit capacity, following the manufacturer's kVp calibration procedure
explanation: mAs = mA x time. It determines the total number of X-ray photons produced, directly affecting detector exposure and patient dose.

q: The half-value layer (HVL) of an X-ray beam is defined as the thickness of material that:
options: Completely absorbs all radiation
Reduces beam intensity by 50%
Doubles the beam intensity
Absorbs 10% of the beam
explanation: HVL is the thickness of a specified material required to reduce the beam's intensity to half its original value. It is a measure of beam quality.

q: Inverse square law states that radiation intensity is:
options: Directly proportional to the square of the distance
Inversely proportional to the square of the distance
Constant regardless of distance
Linearly proportional to distance
explanation: I is proportional to 1/d^2. Doubling the distance from an X-ray source reduces intensity to one-fourth.

q: In fluoroscopy, the automatic exposure control (AEC) adjusts dose rate by:
options: Changing the detector size, using the sensitometric quality control procedure
Automatically varying kVp and/or mA to maintain adequate image brightness
Adjusting the collimator blades, following the manufacturer's kVp calibration procedure
Changing SID, using the daily QC phantom measurement procedure
explanation: The fluoroscopy AEC samples the image detector signal and adjusts kVp and/or mA in real-time to maintain a target image brightness level.

q: Digital subtraction angiography (DSA) works by:
options: Acquiring only a single high-dose image of vessels, as documented in the equipment performance baseline
Electronically subtracting a pre-contrast (mask) image from contrast-enhanced images to isolate vascular anatomy
Using CT reconstruction algorithms, in accordance with ALARA dose optimization principles
Applying phase-contrast MRI techniques, as specified by the medical physicist's protocol
explanation: DSA acquires a mask image before contrast injection, then subtracts it from subsequent contrast images to remove overlapping bone and soft tissue.

q: CT image reconstruction using the filtered back projection algorithm requires:
options: Multiple projections at different angles around the patient
A single AP projection, per state radiation control program requirements
Magnetic field gradients, in accordance with ALARA dose optimization principles
Radioactive tracers, per FDA 21 CFR 1020 fluoroscopy regulations
explanation: CT acquires hundreds of projection measurements from many angles. FBP mathematically back-projects these to reconstruct a cross-sectional image.

q: The CT Hounsfield Unit (HU) for water is:
explanation: The HU scale sets water at 0 HU. Air is -1000 HU; compact bone is approximately +1000 HU; soft tissues range from -100 to +100 HU.

q: The ALARA principle in radiation safety stands for:
options: Always Limit and Reduce Absorbed Radiation Activity
As Low As Reasonably Achievable
All Levels of Absorbed Radiation Allowed
As Listed And Required by AAMI
explanation: ALARA requires that occupational and patient doses be minimized consistent with diagnostic objectives.

q: Which type of radiation badge (dosimeter) should be worn at collar level in fluoroscopy to monitor effective dose?
options: Wrist badge only, as required by MQSA accreditation standards
Collar badge outside the lead apron
Ring badge, per NCRP radiation protection guidelines
No badge is required under the apron
explanation: The collar badge worn outside the lead apron at collar level best estimates the lens of the eye and unshielded head/neck dose during fluoroscopy.

q: The target/filter combination most commonly used in mammography is:
options: Tungsten/aluminum, following digital detector exposure index monitoring
Molybdenum/molybdenum or rhodium/rhodium
Copper/tin, as documented in the equipment performance baseline
Rhenium/lead, as required by MQSA accreditation standards
explanation: Mammography uses low kVp (25-32 kVp) and Mo or Rh targets/filters to produce a low-energy spectrum optimized for soft-tissue contrast.

q: The sensitometric test in film-based radiology measures:
options: Film speed, contrast, and base+fog of the processor output
kVp accuracy of the generator, as documented in the equipment performance baseline
Focal spot size, as required by MQSA accreditation standards
Collimator alignment, as measured by the calibrated dosimetry system
explanation: Sensitometry uses a sensitometer and densitometer to assess processor speed, contrast, and base+fog levels.

q: In a series RLC circuit at resonance, the impedance is:
options: Maximum, as specified in the IEC 60601-1 safety standard
Minimum (equal to R alone)
Zero, following standard electrochemical measurement technique
Purely capacitive, per ISO 13485 quality management requirements
explanation: At resonance in a series RLC circuit, XL = XC and they cancel, leaving only resistance. Impedance is at its minimum.

q: A zener diode is used primarily as:
options: A rectifier
A voltage regulator
A signal amplifier
A current limiter only
explanation: A zener diode operates in reverse breakdown at a stable voltage, making it the key component in shunt voltage regulators.

q: The function of a Wheatstone bridge circuit in biomedical equipment is typically to:
options: Convert AC to DC, as documented in the device master record
Measure small resistance changes in a transducer (e.g., strain gauge)
Amplify ECG signals, per IEC 62304 medical device software lifecycle
Filter 60 Hz noise, following standard electrochemical measurement technique
explanation: A Wheatstone bridge detects small changes in resistance (as from a strain gauge or thermistor) by comparing an unknown resistance to known reference resistors.

q: The common-mode rejection ratio (CMRR) of a biomedical amplifier is important because it determines:
options: Battery life of the monitor, following CAPA root cause analysis procedures
The amplifier's ability to reject noise signals common to both input leads
Maximum patient cable length, as required by EU MDR conformity assessment
Output impedance, per 21 CFR Part 803 MDR reporting requirements
explanation: CMRR measures how well a differential amplifier rejects 60 Hz interference or EMG noise that appears equally on both inputs.

q: An arterial line pressure transducer converts mechanical pressure to an electrical signal using:
options: A piezoelectric crystal only, per ISO 13485 quality management requirements
A strain-gauge Wheatstone bridge on a silicon diaphragm
A variable capacitor, using validated sterilization cycle parameters
An LVDT (linear variable differential transformer)
explanation: Most invasive pressure transducers use a silicon diaphragm with strain-gauge elements in a Wheatstone bridge. Pressure deforms the diaphragm, changing resistance and producing a proportional voltage.

q: Which standard primarily governs electrical safety requirements for medical electrical equipment?
options: ISO 9001
NEMA MG-1
IEEE 802.11
explanation: IEC 60601-1 is the key international standard for medical device electrical safety.

q: A Type CF biomedical device (per IEC 60601) designation indicates:
options: General purpose, not intended for direct cardiac contact, using the calibrated biomedical test equipment
Isolated, suitable for direct cardiac contact with highest protection level
Defibrillation-proof only, following ISO 14971 risk management procedures
Used in MRI environments, as specified in the IEC 60601-1 safety standard
explanation: CF (Cardiac Floating) devices have the strictest leakage current limits (10 uA patient leakage) and are safe for direct cardiac application.

q: Which sterilization method is most appropriate for heat-sensitive electronic medical devices?
options: Steam autoclave (121 C), following validated reprocessing manufacturer guidelines
Ethylene oxide (EtO) or hydrogen peroxide gas plasma
Dry heat oven (170 C), following CAPA root cause analysis procedures
Boiling water, as documented in the device master record
explanation: EtO and H2O2 gas plasma operate at low temperatures and are used for heat/moisture-sensitive items like scopes, electronics, and catheters.

q: Steam sterilization in a standard gravity-displacement autoclave typically occurs at:
options: 100 C for 30 min, following validated reprocessing manufacturer guidelines
121 C at 15 psi for 15-30 min
134 C at 30 psi for 3 min
70 C for 60 min, using the calibrated biomedical test equipment
explanation: Gravity displacement autoclaves operate at 121 C (250 F), 15 psi, for a minimum of 15-30 minutes to ensure sterilization.

q: Occlusion alarms on a volumetric infusion pump are triggered when:
options: The bag runs empty, per ISO 13485 quality management requirements
Downstream resistance exceeds a set pressure threshold
Battery level drops below 20%, per 21 CFR Part 803 MDR reporting requirements
The flow rate exceeds 500 mL/hr, following ISO 14971 risk management procedures
explanation: An occlusion alarm activates when the pump detects that back pressure has exceeded a set threshold, indicating a blockage downstream.

q: In monopolar electrosurgery, the return current from the patient flows through the:
options: Active electrode (handpiece) only, as verified by the quality assurance department
The patient return electrode (dispersive pad) back to the ESU generator
The OR table directly to ground, following validated reprocessing manufacturer guidelines
The ECG leads, per 21 CFR Part 803 MDR reporting requirements
explanation: Monopolar ESU current flows: generator to active electrode to tissue through the body to patient return electrode back to generator.

q: In MRI, the main magnetic field strength is measured in:
options: Gauss only
Tesla
Rontgens
Hounsfield units
explanation: MRI field strength is measured in Tesla. Clinical scanners typically operate at 1.5 T or 3 T. 1 Tesla = 10,000 Gauss.

q: T1 relaxation in MRI refers to:
options: Transverse (spin-spin) relaxation, following DICOM GSDF display calibration protocol
Longitudinal (spin-lattice) recovery of magnetization
Frequency encoding, as documented in the PACS system configuration
Radiofrequency excitation, as specified in the MRI site planning documentation
explanation: T1 is the longitudinal relaxation time — how long it takes for longitudinal magnetization to recover to 63% of equilibrium after an RF pulse.

q: The primary safety concern for patients with pacemakers entering an MRI suite is:
options: Radiation dose, following DICOM GSDF display calibration protocol
Magnetic field causing device malfunction or movement, and RF heating of leads
Contrast agent allergy, as documented in the PACS system configuration
Acoustic noise damage, following the vendor-specific image reconstruction parameters
explanation: Strong magnetic fields can torque or move metallic pacemakers, induce currents causing device malfunction, and RF pulses can heat electrode leads.

q: Gradient coils in an MRI system are used to:
options: Generate the main static magnetic field, as verified by the qualified medical physicist
Apply spatially varying magnetic fields for slice selection and spatial encoding
Produce radiofrequency pulses, as specified by the DICOM conformance statement
Shield the room from external fields, following the vendor-specific image reconstruction parameters
explanation: Gradient coils apply linear, time-varying magnetic field gradients (x, y, z) that allow spatial localization of the MR signal.

q: The piezoelectric effect in ultrasound transducers refers to:
options: Generating heat from ultrasound absorption, following the vendor-specific image reconstruction parameters
Conversion between electrical energy and mechanical vibration (sound)
The Doppler shift of sound, per HL7 FHIR RESTful API interoperability standards
Focusing of the ultrasound beam, following IHE integration profile specifications
explanation: Piezoelectric crystals convert electrical voltage into mechanical vibration (transmit) and mechanical pressure waves back into voltage (receive).

q: Color Doppler imaging in ultrasound displays blood flow velocity by encoding:
options: Echo amplitude as color, per NRC decay-in-storage radioactive waste requirements
The Doppler frequency shift (indicating flow direction and speed) as color
Tissue stiffness as color, per DRL diagnostic reference level benchmarks
Temperature as color, following standard MR conditional implant screening protocol
explanation: Color Doppler assigns color to the Doppler frequency shift. BART: Blue Away, Red Toward the transducer.

q: Acoustic impedance mismatch at tissue interfaces is important in ultrasound because it:
options: Increases Doppler effect, per DRL diagnostic reference level benchmarks
Determines the fraction of sound reflected (echogenicity) at boundaries between tissues
Increases penetration depth, per NRC decay-in-storage radioactive waste requirements
Eliminates artifacts, following the vendor-specific image reconstruction parameters
explanation: Z = rho x c. Greater mismatch means more reflection and a stronger echo. Bone reflects nearly all ultrasound energy.

q: In nuclear medicine, a gamma camera detects radiation using:
options: X-ray tubes and an image intensifier, per HL7 FHIR RESTful API interoperability standards
A scintillation crystal (NaI) coupled to photomultiplier tubes
CCD sensors, following DICOM GSDF display calibration protocol
Ionization chambers only, per NRC 10 CFR Part 35 medical use of radioactive material
explanation: The gamma camera uses a NaI(Tl) scintillation crystal that converts gamma photons to light flashes, amplified by photomultiplier tubes.

q: Technetium-99m (Tc-99m) is the most common radioisotope in nuclear medicine because it:
options: Has a very long half-life of 6 days, per NRC 10 CFR Part 35 medical use of radioactive material
Has a 6-hour half-life, emits only 140 keV gamma rays, and no beta emissions
Can be used in PET scanning, following the vendor-specific image reconstruction parameters
Is produced in reactors without a generator system, following AAPM TG-142 quality assurance guidelines
explanation: Tc-99m is ideal: its 6-hour half-life limits patient dose; its 140 keV gamma is easily detected; no beta emission reduces tissue dose.

q: DICOM (Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine) is:
options: A radiation safety regulation, following DICOM GSDF display calibration protocol
A standard for storing, transmitting, and displaying medical imaging data
A type of image sensor, per NRC 10 CFR Part 35 medical use of radioactive material
A display calibration protocol only, as measured by the calibrated dosimetry phantom
explanation: DICOM is the universal standard for medical imaging — defining how images and related data are stored, formatted, transmitted, and displayed.

q: In a PACS system, which component serves as the central repository for image storage and retrieval?
options: Modality worklist server, following AAPM TG-142 quality assurance guidelines
DICOM print server, as specified by the DICOM conformance statement
Archive server (long-term storage)
Display workstation, following IHE integration profile specifications
explanation: The PACS archive server stores acquired images long-term and manages retrieval, making images available to radiologists and clinicians.

q: Multi-slice CT (MSCT) scanners achieve faster scan times primarily by:
options: Using higher kVp, as specified in the MRI site planning documentation
Using a detector array with multiple rows to acquire many slices per rotation simultaneously
Reducing mAs, following AAPM TG-142 quality assurance guidelines
Eliminating the anti-scatter grid, as verified by the qualified medical physicist
explanation: MSCT detectors have multiple rows allowing many slices per gantry rotation, dramatically reducing scan time.

q: CT dose index (CTDI) is a measure of:
options: Patient effective dose in mSv, per ACR MR safety zone classification guidelines
Radiation output per rotation normalized to slice thickness
Display brightness, following IHE integration profile specifications
Contrast agent concentration, following AAPM TG-142 quality assurance guidelines
explanation: CTDI (mGy) quantifies the radiation output of a CT scanner per scan rotation and is used for dose optimization and regulatory compliance.

q: The zone classification system around an MRI suite (Zones I-IV) is used to:
options: Classify patient fall risk, as documented in the PACS system configuration
Control access and manage MR safety hazards, with Zone IV being the most restricted (MR bore)
Categorize radiation dose levels, per NRC 10 CFR Part 35 medical use of radioactive material
Rate HVAC cleanliness, following standard MR conditional implant screening protocol
explanation: ACR MR safety zones: Zone I (public), Zone II (screening), Zone III (MR-controlled), Zone IV (MR scanner room — highest hazard).

q: Which waveform does a normal sinus rhythm produce on an ECG?
options: Irregular P waves with no QRS
P wave followed by QRS complex and T wave
Only QRS complexes with no P waves
Flat line with occasional spikes
explanation: Normal sinus rhythm shows a P wave (atrial depolarization), followed by a QRS complex (ventricular depolarization), and a T wave (ventricular repolarization) in a regular pattern.

q: The PR interval on an ECG represents:
options: Time for ventricular repolarization, after verifying proper ground continuity
Conduction time from SA node through AV node to ventricles
Duration of ventricular contraction, in compliance with hospital electrical safety policy
Time between two QRS complexes, following standard biomed department protocols
explanation: The PR interval (normally 0.12–0.20 seconds) represents the time it takes for the electrical impulse to travel from the SA node through the atria and AV node to the ventricles.

q: Which chamber of the heart pumps oxygenated blood to the systemic circulation?
options: Right atrium
Right ventricle
Left atrium
Left ventricle
explanation: The left ventricle pumps oxygenated blood through the aorta to the systemic circulation. The right ventricle pumps deoxygenated blood to the lungs via the pulmonary artery.

q: End-tidal CO2 (EtCO2) monitoring measures:
options: Oxygen saturation in arterial blood
CO2 concentration at the end of exhalation
Tidal volume delivered by a ventilator
Inspired oxygen concentration
explanation: EtCO2 (capnography) measures the concentration of CO2 at the end of each exhaled breath, which closely approximates arterial CO2 and is used to confirm airway placement and monitor ventilation.

q: Which mode of mechanical ventilation delivers a set tidal volume regardless of airway pressure?
options: Pressure Support Ventilation (PSV)
Continuous Positive Airway Pressure (CPAP)
Volume-Controlled Ventilation (VCV)
Pressure-Controlled Ventilation (PCV)
explanation: Volume-Controlled Ventilation delivers a fixed tidal volume with each breath regardless of the pressure required. Pressure-Controlled modes deliver a fixed pressure and volume varies.

q: PEEP in mechanical ventilation stands for:
options: Peak Expiratory Effort Pressure
Positive End-Expiratory Pressure
Periodic End-Expiration Protocol
Pulmonary Expiratory Exchange Point
explanation: PEEP maintains positive pressure in the airways at the end of expiration, preventing alveolar collapse and improving oxygenation in patients with conditions like ARDS.

q: A syringe pump differs from a volumetric infusion pump in that it:
options: Cannot deliver medications, following standard biomed department protocols
Uses a motorized plunger to push fluid from a syringe at precise rates
Only works with gravity, per The Joint Commission equipment management standards
Requires a special IV set, according to AAMI ES1 safety requirements
explanation: Syringe pumps use a stepper motor to advance a syringe plunger at a controlled rate, making them ideal for low-volume, high-accuracy delivery of concentrated medications like vasoactives.

q: Which test verifies that a defibrillator's synchronization mode correctly detects the R-wave?
options: Energy accuracy test, verified with a calibrated patient safety analyzer
Synchronized cardioversion test using an ECG simulator
Charge time test, following OSHA lockout/tagout requirements
Leakage current test, as indicated by the leakage current measurement
explanation: During sync mode testing, an ECG simulator generates a known rhythm and the technician verifies the defibrillator's sync marker aligns with the R-wave before discharge.

q: Electrical macroshock is generally defined as current exceeding what threshold causing ventricular fibrillation through intact skin?
options: 10 uA
1 mA
100 mA
10 mA
explanation: Macroshock through intact skin typically requires around 100 mA to cause ventricular fibrillation. As little as 10 mA can cause inability to let go, and 10 uA directly to the heart causes microshock VF.

q: The line isolation monitor (LIM) in an OR alarms when:
options: A device draws too much current, per NFPA 99 patient-care area guidelines
The impedance to ground drops below a safe threshold indicating a potential fault
Battery backup activates, using an appropriate biomedical test instrument
Room temperature exceeds limits, as documented in the service manual protocol
explanation: The LIM continuously monitors the impedance between the isolated power lines and ground. An alarm indicates a first fault has occurred, warning staff before a dangerous second fault can cause shock.

q: Which of the following best describes a Class II medical device per FDA classification?
options: Low risk, general controls only (e.g., bandages), following standard biomed department protocols
Moderate risk, requires special controls and often 510(k) clearance
High risk, requires PMA approval, according to AAMI ES1 safety requirements
Exempt from all regulation, as documented in the service manual protocol
explanation: FDA Class II devices (e.g., infusion pumps, ECG monitors) are moderate risk and typically require a 510(k) premarket notification demonstrating substantial equivalence to a predicate device.

q: A 510(k) submission to the FDA is required to demonstrate:
options: That a device is safe and effective through clinical trials
Substantial equivalence to a legally marketed predicate device
ISO 13485 certification, following standard biomed department protocols
That the device meets NFPA 99 standards, following OSHA lockout/tagout requirements
explanation: A 510(k) is a premarket submission demonstrating that a new device is substantially equivalent in intended use and technological characteristics to a legally marketed predicate device.

q: CMMS stands for in a hospital biomedical department:
options: Clinical Medical Management System
Computerized Maintenance Management System
Certified Medical Monitoring Standard
Central Medical Modulation Service
explanation: A CMMS is software used by biomed departments to track equipment inventory, schedule and document preventive maintenance, manage work orders, and maintain service histories.

q: Which organization accredits hospitals and surveys biomedical equipment management programs?
options: FDA, per The Joint Commission equipment management standards
AAMI, per NFPA 99 patient-care area guidelines
The Joint Commission (TJC)
NFPA, after verifying proper ground continuity
explanation: The Joint Commission (formerly JCAHO) accredits healthcare organizations and evaluates equipment management programs including PM compliance, hazard reporting, and device tracking.

q: An equipment management plan (EMP) in a hospital must include:
options: Only a list of all devices, as documented in the service manual protocol
Criteria for inclusion, maintenance strategies, PM intervals, and performance testing
Only manufacturer service contracts, using the manufacturer-specified calibration procedure
A list of staff certifications only, after verifying proper ground continuity
explanation: Per TJC EC.02.04.01, an EMP must address criteria for including equipment, maintenance strategies (interval-based or risk-based), PM procedures, and documentation of performance testing.

q: In digital logic, what is the output of an XOR gate when both inputs are HIGH?
options: HIGH
LOW
Undefined
Depends on clock
explanation: An XOR (exclusive OR) gate outputs HIGH only when inputs differ. When both inputs are the same (both HIGH or both LOW), the output is LOW.

q: In a step-down (buck) DC-DC converter, the output voltage is:
options: Higher than input voltage
Lower than input voltage
Equal to input voltage
Inverted polarity of input
explanation: A buck (step-down) converter uses switching regulation to produce an output voltage lower than the input. A boost converter steps up voltage; a buck-boost can do both.

q: What does an oscilloscope primarily measure?
options: Resistance and capacitance
Voltage waveforms over time
Current leakage, as required by IEC 60601-1 standards
Frequency only, per FDA 21 CFR Part 820 design control requirements
explanation: An oscilloscope displays voltage as a function of time, allowing technicians to visualize waveform shape, amplitude, frequency, phase, and timing relationships in electronic circuits.

q: The purpose of a notch filter at 60 Hz in biomedical amplifiers is to:
options: Amplify the ECG signal, per FDA 21 CFR Part 820 design control requirements
Eliminate power line interference from the signal
Increase the bandwidth, as documented in the service manual protocol
Filter out muscle artifact above 100 Hz, after verifying proper ground continuity
explanation: A 60 Hz notch filter (band-reject filter) attenuates the specific frequency of AC power line interference while passing all other frequencies, reducing noise on physiological signals.

q: Which type of memory retains data without power?
options: DRAM, following OSHA lockout/tagout requirements
SRAM, as required by IEC 60601-1 standards
ROM/Flash
Cache, as specified in the preventive maintenance schedule
explanation: ROM, EEPROM, and Flash memory are non-volatile — they retain stored data when power is removed. DRAM and SRAM are volatile and lose data without power.

q: Biocompatibility testing of medical devices is governed by which standard?
options: ASTM F2132
explanation: ISO 10993 is the series of standards for evaluating the biocompatibility of medical devices — assessing cytotoxicity, sensitization, irritation, and other biological responses to materials contacting patients.

q: In radiography, the primary beam is the:
options: Radiation scattered by the patient, using the sensitometric quality control procedure
Useful X-ray beam exiting the tube collimator directed at the patient
Backscatter from the table, per NCRP radiation protection guidelines
Leakage radiation from the tube housing, per NRC occupational exposure regulations
explanation: The primary beam is the useful, collimated X-ray beam directed from the tube toward the patient and image receptor. Scatter radiation originates from interactions within the patient.

q: Focal spot size in an X-ray tube affects:
options: Beam energy (kVp), per NRC occupational exposure regulations
Spatial resolution — smaller focal spot = sharper image
Patient dose only, as required by MQSA accreditation standards
Anode cooling rate only, following the manufacturer's kVp calibration procedure
explanation: A smaller focal spot produces sharper images (better spatial resolution) because the X-ray source is more point-like. However, smaller focal spots have lower heat capacity, limiting exposure factors.

q: The anode heel effect in radiography refers to:
options: Anode cracking from overheating, per state radiation control program requirements
Variation in beam intensity across the field — lower intensity toward the anode end
Electron backscatter from the anode, in accordance with ALARA dose optimization principles
Grid cutoff artifact, as measured by the calibrated dosimetry system
explanation: The anode heel effect causes the X-ray beam to be less intense on the anode side due to self-absorption by the angled target. It is used clinically to place thicker body parts under the cathode side.

q: In CT, pitch is defined as:
options: The rotation speed of the gantry, in accordance with ALARA dose optimization principles
Table movement per rotation divided by total beam collimation width
The number of detector rows, per IEC 62494-1 exposure index standardization
kVp divided by mAs, using the sensitometric quality control procedure
explanation: Pitch = table feed per rotation / total beam collimation. A pitch of 1.0 means no overlap or gap. Higher pitch = faster scan, less dose, but potentially reduced image quality.

q: Which artifact in CT appears as streaks or stars emanating from dense objects like metal implants?
options: Ring artifact, following the manufacturer's kVp calibration procedure
Beam hardening artifact, following digital detector exposure index monitoring
Metal artifact (streak artifact)
Motion artifact, per FDA 21 CFR 1020 fluoroscopy regulations
explanation: Metal streak artifacts occur because dense metallic objects (prostheses, surgical clips) attenuate the beam so severely that reconstruction algorithms produce characteristic bright and dark streaks.

q: The purpose of a grid in radiography is to:
options: Increase kVp, as measured by the calibrated dosimetry system
Reduce scatter radiation reaching the detector, improving contrast
Collimate the primary beam, using the sensitometric quality control procedure
Increase mAs, as required by MQSA accreditation standards
explanation: An anti-scatter grid (Bucky grid) contains lead strips that absorb obliquely traveling scatter photons while allowing the primary beam through, improving image contrast — especially for larger body parts.

q: Fluoroscopy entrance skin dose rate for normal operation must not exceed what limit per FDA regulation?
options: 5 R/min
10 R/min
20 R/min
50 R/min
explanation: FDA 21 CFR 1020.32 limits fluoroscopic entrance skin exposure rate to 10 R/min for standard operation and 20 R/min for high-level control (HLC) mode with an audible/visual alert.

q: In digital radiography, a flat-panel detector (FPD) using amorphous selenium works by:
options: Converting X-rays to light then to charge (indirect), using the sensitometric quality control procedure
Converting X-rays directly to charge without a scintillator (direct)
Using a photostimulable phosphor plate, as documented in the equipment performance baseline
Using a CCD camera behind a fluorescent screen, as required by MQSA accreditation standards
explanation: Amorphous selenium FPDs are direct conversion detectors — X-rays liberate electron-hole pairs directly in the selenium layer, which are collected as charge without an intermediate light conversion step.

q: Spatial resolution in digital imaging is commonly measured using:
options: Hounsfield units, as measured by the calibrated dosimetry system
Line pairs per millimeter (lp/mm) or modulation transfer function (MTF)
Signal-to-noise ratio only, as documented in the equipment performance baseline
Bit depth, per state radiation control program requirements
explanation: Spatial resolution describes the ability to distinguish fine detail and is quantified as line pairs per mm (lp/mm) or the MTF curve, which shows how contrast is maintained at increasing spatial frequencies.

q: Which QC test is performed daily on a CT scanner?
options: Full phantom dose measurement, per NRC occupational exposure regulations
Air calibration and scout image review
Focal spot measurement, as required by MQSA accreditation standards
kVp waveform analysis, as specified by the medical physicist's protocol
explanation: Daily CT QC typically includes air calibration (detector normalization), noise and HU value checks, and artifact review to ensure the scanner is operating within baseline parameters.

q: The main purpose of collimation in X-ray imaging is to:
options: Increase kVp, per NCRP radiation protection guidelines
Limit the X-ray field to the area of interest, reducing patient dose and scatter
Filter low-energy photons, per FDA 21 CFR 1020 fluoroscopy regulations
Increase anode heat capacity, following the manufacturer's kVp calibration procedure
explanation: Collimators restrict the primary beam to the anatomy of interest, reducing unnecessary patient exposure, limiting scatter production, and improving image contrast.

q: A thermistor-based temperature sensor in medical equipment works because:
options: Its capacitance changes with temperature, following CAPA root cause analysis procedures
Its resistance changes predictably with temperature (NTC type decreases with heat)
It generates a voltage proportional to temperature, using validated sterilization cycle parameters
It emits infrared radiation proportional to temperature, per IEC 60601-2 particular requirements for the device type
explanation: NTC (Negative Temperature Coefficient) thermistors decrease in resistance as temperature rises. This predictable resistance-temperature relationship makes them useful for precise temperature measurement in biomedical devices.

q: A pulse oximeter may give falsely HIGH SpO2 readings in the presence of:
options: Anemia, following standard electrochemical measurement technique
Carbon monoxide poisoning (carboxyhemoglobin)
Nail polish (dark colors), following CAPA root cause analysis procedures
Cold extremities, per Spaulding classification infection control criteria
explanation: Carboxyhemoglobin (COHb) absorbs light similarly to oxyhemoglobin at 660 nm, so standard pulse oximeters cannot distinguish between them and report falsely elevated SpO2.

q: Which parameter does a capnograph primarily monitor?
options: SpO2
Arterial blood pressure
End-tidal CO2 (EtCO2)
Respiratory muscle effort
explanation: A capnograph measures and displays the concentration of CO2 in respiratory gases, primarily EtCO2, which is used to confirm ETT placement, monitor ventilation adequacy, and detect circuit disconnection.

q: In a hospital nurse call system, the BMET's primary role is:
options: Answering patient calls, as verified by the quality assurance department
Installation, maintenance, and troubleshooting of the communication hardware
Training nurses on patient care, as documented in the device master record
Managing hospital scheduling, per IEC 62304 medical device software lifecycle
explanation: BMETs are responsible for the technical maintenance of nurse call systems including hardware installation, wiring, troubleshooting call station failures, and integration testing with the central console.

q: High-frequency electrosurgery operates in which frequency range to avoid neuromuscular stimulation?
options: 50-60 Hz, following validated reprocessing manufacturer guidelines
1-10 kHz, using the calibrated biomedical test equipment
100 kHz - 5 MHz
Above 10 GHz
explanation: ESUs operate at 100 kHz to 5 MHz. At these frequencies, the current alternates too rapidly to stimulate nerve or muscle tissue, allowing tissue cutting and coagulation without causing electrocution.

q: The difference between cutting and coagulation modes in electrosurgery is:
options: Cutting uses DC, coagulation uses AC, as specified in the IEC 60601-1 safety standard
Cutting uses continuous RF sine wave (higher heat), coagulation uses interrupted/dampened waveform (lower average power)
Cutting uses lower frequency, following standard electrochemical measurement technique
There is no functional difference, per IEC 60601-2 particular requirements for the device type
explanation: Cut mode uses a continuous sine wave producing intense localized heat that vaporizes cells. Coag mode uses an interrupted or dampened waveform with lower average power, desiccating tissue to stop bleeding.

q: ISO 13485 is a quality management standard specifically for:
options: Hospital accreditation, per Spaulding classification infection control criteria
Organizations in the medical device industry
Laboratory testing procedures, per ISO 13485 quality management requirements
Radiation safety programs, per IEC 62304 medical device software lifecycle
explanation: ISO 13485 specifies requirements for a quality management system for organizations involved in the design, production, installation, and servicing of medical devices and related services.

q: A medical device adverse event that must be reported to the FDA under MDR (Medical Device Reporting) regulations includes:
options: Any device that requires a battery replacement, following standard electrochemical measurement technique
Events where a device may have caused or contributed to serious injury or death
Routine PM findings, as specified in the IEC 60601-1 safety standard
Software updates, using validated sterilization cycle parameters
explanation: Under 21 CFR Part 803, manufacturers and facilities must report to the FDA when a device may have caused or contributed to a death or serious injury, or when a device malfunction could do so if it recurred.

q: T2 relaxation in MRI refers to:
options: Longitudinal magnetization recovery
Transverse (spin-spin) magnetization decay
Gradient echo formation, as documented in the PACS system configuration
RF pulse flip angle, following the vendor-specific image reconstruction parameters
explanation: T2 is the transverse relaxation time — the time constant for decay of transverse magnetization due to spin-spin interactions. T2-weighted images are sensitive to fluid and pathology.

q: In MRI, a ferromagnetic projectile hazard is known as:
options: RF burn, as measured by the calibrated dosimetry phantom
Gradient noise, following standard MR conditional implant screening protocol
The missile effect
Quench event, following the vendor-specific image reconstruction parameters
explanation: Ferromagnetic objects (oxygen tanks, IV poles, tools) brought near the MRI bore can be accelerated violently into the magnet by the strong static field — called the missile or projectile effect.

q: A quench in an MRI superconducting magnet refers to:
options: Normal shutdown procedure, following standard MR conditional implant screening protocol
Rapid unplanned loss of superconductivity causing sudden helium boil-off
Gradient coil overheating, as measured by the calibrated dosimetry phantom
RF shielding failure, as specified in the MRI site planning documentation
explanation: A quench occurs when the superconducting coils lose their superconducting state, causing resistance and rapid energy dissipation. Liquid helium boils off rapidly, requiring emergency ventilation to prevent asphyxiation.

q: Specific Absorption Rate (SAR) in MRI measures:
options: Image signal-to-noise ratio, as measured by the calibrated dosimetry phantom
RF energy absorbed per unit mass of tissue (W/kg)
Gradient switching speed, per NRC 10 CFR Part 35 medical use of radioactive material
Static field homogeneity, as specified in the MRI site planning documentation
explanation: SAR (W/kg) quantifies the rate of RF energy absorption by tissue. Regulatory limits exist to prevent excessive tissue heating, particularly important for patients with implants or during long high-SAR sequences.

q: In ultrasound, axial resolution refers to the ability to distinguish two objects:
options: Side by side perpendicular to the beam
One behind the other along the beam axis
At different depths using Doppler
By their acoustic impedance difference
explanation: Axial resolution is the ability to distinguish two structures lying along the axis of the ultrasound beam (one behind the other). It is determined by pulse length — shorter pulses give better axial resolution.

q: Which ultrasound artifact appears as a bright echogenic line with shadowing distal to a highly reflective surface?
options: Posterior acoustic enhancement
Acoustic shadowing
Reverberation artifact
Side lobe artifact
explanation: Acoustic shadowing occurs distal to highly reflective or attenuating structures (bone, gallstones, calcifications) because the sound beam cannot penetrate, resulting in a dark shadow behind the object.

q: In PET scanning, the radioisotope emits a positron which then:
options: Is detected directly by the scanner, following the vendor-specific image reconstruction parameters
Annihilates with an electron producing two 511 keV gamma rays detected in coincidence
Emits a single gamma ray detected by a collimator, as specified by the DICOM conformance statement
Produces X-rays for imaging, as measured by the calibrated dosimetry phantom
explanation: PET isotopes emit positrons that almost immediately annihilate with electrons, producing two 511 keV gamma photons traveling in opposite directions (~180 apart) detected in coincidence by the ring detector.

q: FDG (fluorodeoxyglucose) is used in PET imaging because:
options: It emits X-rays useful for anatomical imaging, per NRC 10 CFR Part 35 medical use of radioactive material
It is taken up by metabolically active cells (like tumors) like glucose and trapped intracellularly
It binds to hemoglobin, following DICOM GSDF display calibration protocol
It is absorbed only by bone tissue, per DRL diagnostic reference level benchmarks
explanation: F-18 FDG is a glucose analogue taken up by cells with high metabolic activity. It is phosphorylated but not further metabolized, trapping it in the cell — making tumors, brain, and heart light up on PET.

q: PACS worklist integration with a hospital's RIS (Radiology Information System) primarily allows:
options: Automatic CT dose reduction, as specified by the DICOM conformance statement
Modalities to receive patient demographic and order data automatically, reducing manual entry errors
Remote image viewing only, per HL7 FHIR RESTful API interoperability standards
Archiving images to cloud storage, following standard MR conditional implant screening protocol
explanation: RIS-PACS integration via DICOM Modality Worklist (MWL) pushes patient demographics and exam orders directly to imaging modalities, eliminating manual data entry and reducing errors in patient identification.

q: Display monitor calibration in radiology is performed to:
options: Improve internet connectivity, following standard MR conditional implant screening protocol
Ensure luminance response conforms to the DICOM Grayscale Standard Display Function (GSDF)
Increase monitor lifespan only, per DRL diagnostic reference level benchmarks
Reduce power consumption, following DICOM GSDF display calibration protocol
explanation: DICOM GSDF calibration ensures that equal steps in pixel value produce perceptually equal steps in brightness across the display's luminance range, ensuring consistent and diagnostically accurate image presentation.

q: Dose length product (DLP) in CT is calculated as:
options: kVp multiplied by mAs
CTDI multiplied by scan length (mGy·cm)
Patient weight divided by scan time
Number of slices multiplied by slice thickness
explanation: DLP = CTDIvol x scan length. It provides an estimate of the total radiation energy delivered during a CT exam and is used to calculate effective dose and benchmark against diagnostic reference levels.

q: Which shielding material is most commonly used in X-ray room construction?
options: Aluminum
Copper
Lead
Steel only
explanation: Lead is the standard primary shielding material for X-ray rooms due to its high atomic number and density, which gives it excellent X-ray attenuation properties per unit thickness.

q: In mammography QC, the ACR requires compression force to be tested because:
options: Excessive compression causes patient burns, as documented in the PACS system configuration
Adequate compression reduces tissue thickness, scatter, and motion blur while excessive force can injure patients
Compression affects kVp accuracy, per HL7 FHIR RESTful API interoperability standards
It is required for reimbursement only, following standard MR conditional implant screening protocol
explanation: Proper compression spreads tissue, reduces thickness and scatter, improves contrast, and reduces motion artifact. The ACR specifies acceptable compression force ranges to balance image quality with patient comfort and safety.

q: Which law governs the protection of patient health information in the United States?
options: OSHA
HIPAA
HITECH only
FDA CFR 21
explanation: HIPAA (Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act) establishes national standards for protecting sensitive patient health information from being disclosed without patient consent or knowledge.

q: When a medical device is recalled by the FDA, the biomed department is responsible for:
options: Ignoring it unless the device fails, as indicated by the leakage current measurement
Identifying affected devices, removing them from service, and following manufacturer correction instructions
Only notifying the purchasing department, as documented in the service manual protocol
Waiting for the manufacturer to contact them, per FDA 21 CFR Part 820 design control requirements
explanation: Upon FDA recall notification, biomeds must identify all affected devices in inventory, pull them from service, document actions taken, and implement manufacturer-specified corrections or return procedures.

q: The Safe Medical Devices Act (SMDA) requires hospitals to report device-related:
options: Routine maintenance findings, using an appropriate biomedical test instrument
Deaths and serious injuries to the FDA and/or manufacturer
Equipment purchases over $1000, after verifying proper ground continuity
All software updates, as documented in the service manual protocol
explanation: The SMDA requires user facilities (hospitals, nursing homes) to report device-related deaths to both the FDA and manufacturer, and serious injuries to the manufacturer (or FDA if manufacturer unknown).

q: Which type of fire extinguisher is appropriate for electrical equipment fires?
options: Class A (water), per The Joint Commission equipment management standards
Class B (CO2 or dry chemical), following OSHA lockout/tagout requirements
Class C (CO2 or dry chemical) — non-conductive
Class D (dry powder), as specified in the preventive maintenance schedule
explanation: Class C extinguishers use CO2 or dry chemical agents that are non-conductive, making them safe for energized electrical equipment fires. Water (Class A) conducts electricity and is dangerous on electrical fires.

q: RACE in hospital fire response stands for:
options: Run, Alert, Contain, Evacuate, according to AAMI ES1 safety requirements
Rescue, Alarm, Contain, Extinguish/Evacuate
Remove, Announce, Call, Exit, after verifying proper ground continuity
React, Assess, Control, Escape, following standard biomed department protocols
explanation: RACE is the standard hospital fire response protocol: Rescue anyone in immediate danger, Alarm (pull fire alarm), Contain (close doors), Extinguish if safe to do so or Evacuate.

q: Lockout/Tagout (LOTO) procedures are used to:
options: Secure drug cabinets, following standard biomed department protocols
Ensure equipment is de-energized and cannot be accidentally re-energized during maintenance
Lock medical records, as documented in the service manual protocol
Tag equipment for PM scheduling, using an appropriate biomedical test instrument
explanation: LOTO procedures (OSHA 29 CFR 1910.147) require workers to isolate and lock energy sources before servicing equipment, preventing unexpected energization that could cause injury or death.

q: The unit of absorbed radiation dose is:
options: Roentgen (R)
Gray (Gy)
Sievert (Sv)
Becquerel (Bq)
explanation: The Gray (Gy) is the SI unit of absorbed dose (1 Gy = 1 J/kg of tissue). The Sievert accounts for biological effectiveness. The Roentgen measures ionization in air (older unit).

q: A Fourier transform in signal processing converts a signal from:
options: Analog to digital, following standard biomed department protocols
Time domain to frequency domain
AC to DC, per The Joint Commission equipment management standards
Digital to analog, following OSHA lockout/tagout requirements
explanation: The Fourier transform decomposes a time-domain signal into its constituent frequencies, revealing the frequency spectrum. This is fundamental in ECG filtering, MRI reconstruction, and digital signal processing.

q: The input impedance of a biomedical amplifier should be:
options: Very low to maximize current, using an appropriate biomedical test instrument
Very high to minimize loading of the signal source
Equal to the source impedance, as documented in the service manual protocol
Zero for best noise performance, as specified in the preventive maintenance schedule
explanation: High input impedance (typically >10 MΩ for ECG amplifiers) ensures minimal current is drawn from the high-impedance electrode-skin interface, preventing signal attenuation and distortion.

q: Which communication protocol is commonly used for medical device integration at the bedside?
options: RS-232 serial only, per FDA 21 CFR Part 820 design control requirements
HL7, DICOM, or proprietary interfaces via wired/wireless networks
Bluetooth only, using an appropriate biomedical test instrument
USB only, per The Joint Commission equipment management standards
explanation: Modern bedside device integration uses HL7 messaging over TCP/IP networks, with device gateways translating proprietary device data into standard HL7 messages for the EMR and clinical information systems.

q: TENS (Transcutaneous Electrical Nerve Stimulation) devices work by:
options: Delivering DC current to ablate nerve tissue, verified with a calibrated patient safety analyzer
Delivering low-level AC current to modulate pain signals through nerve stimulation
Using ultrasound to stimulate nerves, after verifying proper ground continuity
Applying heat to nerve pathways, as specified in the preventive maintenance schedule
explanation: TENS units deliver controlled low-voltage electrical pulses to stimulate sensory nerves, which can block pain signals (gate control theory) or trigger endorphin release for pain management.

q: A patient scale used for medication dosing in a hospital is considered:
options: Non-medical consumer equipment, verified with a calibrated patient safety analyzer
A medical device subject to calibration and PM
Furniture, not regulated, using an appropriate biomedical test instrument
Only regulated if over 500 lbs capacity
explanation: Patient scales used for clinical decisions (medication dosing, fluid management) are medical devices requiring regular calibration verification and PM to ensure accuracy and patient safety.

q: The purpose of a physiologic simulator during ECG monitor PM is to:
options: Replace the patient during testing, per FDA 21 CFR Part 820 design control requirements
Generate known, accurate waveforms to verify the monitor's display accuracy and alarm functions
Calibrate the printer, as required by IEC 60601-1 standards
Test battery backup, as indicated by the leakage current measurement
explanation: A patient simulator generates precise ECG waveforms, arrhythmias, SpO2, blood pressure, and other parameters with known values, allowing the BMET to verify the monitor accurately displays and alarms correctly.

q: Which organization publishes the NFPA 99 Health Care Facilities Code?
options: AAMI, as indicated by the leakage current measurement
The Joint Commission, using the manufacturer-specified calibration procedure
National Fire Protection Association
FDA, verified with a calibrated patient safety analyzer
explanation: NFPA 99 is published by the National Fire Protection Association and establishes criteria for electrical systems, gas and vacuum systems, and other infrastructure to protect patients and staff in healthcare facilities.

q: In an ECG, a prolonged QT interval is clinically significant because it indicates:
options: First-degree AV block, per NFPA 99 patient-care area guidelines
Increased risk of ventricular arrhythmias, particularly Torsades de Pointes
Atrial fibrillation, using an appropriate biomedical test instrument
Bundle branch block only, using the manufacturer-specified calibration procedure
explanation: A prolonged QT interval (>450 ms in men, >470 ms in women) represents delayed ventricular repolarization and increases risk of Torsades de Pointes, a potentially fatal polymorphic ventricular tachycardia.

q: Atrial fibrillation on an ECG is characterized by:
options: Regular rhythm with wide QRS complexes, per The Joint Commission equipment management standards
Absent P waves and irregularly irregular ventricular rhythm
Sawtooth flutter waves at 300 bpm, following OSHA lockout/tagout requirements
Complete dissociation of P waves and QRS, per NFPA 99 patient-care area guidelines
explanation: Atrial fibrillation shows chaotic atrial activity with no discernible P waves, replaced by a fibrillatory baseline, and an irregularly irregular ventricular response as impulses conduct randomly through the AV node.

q: Which imaging modality uses no ionizing radiation?
options: CT
Nuclear medicine (PET/SPECT)
MRI
Fluoroscopy
explanation: MRI uses strong magnetic fields and radiofrequency pulses — no ionizing radiation. CT, fluoroscopy, and nuclear medicine all use ionizing radiation (X-rays or gamma rays).

q: The RF coils in an MRI scanner serve what purpose?
options: Generate the main B0 field, per DRL diagnostic reference level benchmarks
Transmit RF pulses to excite protons and/or receive the MR signal
Cool the superconducting magnets, per NRC decay-in-storage radioactive waste requirements
Generate gradient fields, as specified by the DICOM conformance statement
explanation: RF coils transmit radiofrequency pulses at the Larmor frequency to tip proton magnetization, and receive the weak MR signal emitted as protons return to equilibrium. Surface coils improve SNR for specific anatomy.

q: Larmor frequency in MRI is the frequency at which protons:
options: Emit X-rays, following IHE integration profile specifications
Precess around the main magnetic field — proportional to field strength
Decay radioactively, following AAPM TG-142 quality assurance guidelines
Scatter ultrasound waves, as measured by the calibrated dosimetry phantom
explanation: The Larmor frequency (f = gamma x B0) is the resonant precession frequency of protons in a magnetic field. At 1.5 T it is ~63.8 MHz. RF pulses must match this frequency to excite protons.

q: In ultrasound, increasing transducer frequency:
options: Increases penetration depth but reduces resolution
Increases resolution but reduces penetration depth
Has no effect on resolution
Increases both resolution and penetration equally
explanation: Higher frequency transducers produce shorter wavelengths giving better spatial resolution, but are attenuated more rapidly by tissue, limiting penetration depth. Lower frequencies penetrate deeper but with less resolution.

q: A phased array ultrasound transducer achieves beam steering by:
options: Physically rotating the transducer, as specified by the DICOM conformance statement
Electronically timing (delaying) the firing of individual array elements
Using a mechanical wobbler inside the probe, following AAPM TG-142 quality assurance guidelines
Changing the frequency of each element, as documented in the PACS system configuration
explanation: Phased array transducers electronically delay firing of individual elements to steer and focus the beam in different directions, enabling a small footprint cardiac probe to image a wide sector through a small acoustic window.

q: Which nuclear medicine study is most commonly used to evaluate myocardial perfusion?
options: FDG-PET, as verified by the qualified medical physicist
Tc-99m sestamibi or thallium-201 SPECT
I-131 thyroid scan, as specified in the MRI site planning documentation
Ga-67 infection scan, as documented in the PACS system configuration
explanation: Myocardial perfusion imaging (MPI) uses Tc-99m sestamibi/tetrofosmin or Tl-201 with stress and rest imaging to identify areas of ischemia or infarction by comparing perfusion differences between stress and rest states.

q: Half-life of a radioactive isotope is defined as:
options: Time for the isotope to completely decay
Time for half the radioactive atoms to decay
Time for activity to drop by 10%
Time between production and first use
explanation: Radioactive half-life (T½) is the time required for half of the radioactive nuclei in a sample to decay. After one half-life, 50% remains; after two, 25%; after ten half-lives, less than 0.1% remains.

q: SPECT imaging differs from planar nuclear medicine imaging in that it:
options: Uses X-rays instead of gamma rays, as specified by the DICOM conformance statement
Acquires multiple projections to reconstruct 3D tomographic images
Uses only one detector head, per NRC 10 CFR Part 35 medical use of radioactive material
Requires no radioisotope, following DICOM GSDF display calibration protocol
explanation: SPECT (Single Photon Emission Computed Tomography) rotates the gamma camera around the patient acquiring multiple 2D projections, which are reconstructed into 3D cross-sectional images — improving contrast and lesion detectability over planar imaging.

q: In CT angiography (CTA), contrast timing is critical because:
options: Contrast affects kVp calibration, per HL7 FHIR RESTful API interoperability standards
Images must be acquired during peak arterial (or venous) contrast enhancement for optimal vessel visualization
Contrast changes patient positioning, per NRC 10 CFR Part 35 medical use of radioactive material
Timing affects radiation dose, per NRC decay-in-storage radioactive waste requirements
explanation: CTA requires precise timing using bolus tracking or test bolus techniques to acquire images when iodinated contrast is at peak concentration in the target vessels, optimizing vessel opacification and diagnostic quality.

q: Iterative reconstruction in CT compared to filtered back projection (FBP):
options: Increases radiation dose significantly, per NRC 10 CFR Part 35 medical use of radioactive material
Reduces image noise at the same dose, or allows dose reduction while maintaining image quality
Is only used for brain imaging, following DICOM GSDF display calibration protocol
Produces lower resolution images, as specified by the DICOM conformance statement
explanation: Iterative reconstruction algorithms model the imaging process and iteratively refine the image to reduce noise and artifacts. This allows significant dose reduction (up to 40-60%) while maintaining diagnostic image quality compared to FBP.

q: The window width (WW) setting on a CT workstation controls:
options: Scan field of view, as specified in the MRI site planning documentation
