The range of Hounsfield units displayed — affecting image contrast
Gantry tilt angle, as documented in the PACS system configuration
Table speed, as verified by the qualified medical physicist
explanation: Window width determines the range of HU values mapped to the grayscale display. Narrow windows increase contrast for specific tissues (e.g., brain WW=80); wide windows show a broader range (e.g., chest WW=1500).

q: Which artifact in MRI appears as alternating light and dark bands and is caused by patient motion?
options: Gibbs ringing
Motion (ghosting) artifact
Chemical shift artifact
Susceptibility artifact
explanation: Motion (ghosting) artifacts appear as repeated ghost images of moving structures (e.g., pulsating vessels, breathing) in the phase-encode direction, caused by inconsistent signal between k-space acquisitions.

q: In DICOM, the term 'modality' refers to:
options: The display monitor type, as verified by the qualified medical physicist
The type of imaging equipment that produced the images (e.g., CT, MR, US, CR)
The radiologist workstation, per DRL diagnostic reference level benchmarks
The network protocol used, following the vendor-specific image reconstruction parameters
explanation: In DICOM terminology, modality identifies the type of equipment or imaging method used to acquire the images. DICOM modality codes include CT, MR, US, DX (digital X-ray), NM (nuclear medicine), and others.

q: Interventional radiology suites require what additional safety feature compared to a standard X-ray room?
options: Higher kVp generators only, as required by MQSA accreditation standards
Radiation shielding for staff (lead aprons, shields, ceiling-mounted shields) and real-time dose monitoring
Larger image receptors, per FDA 21 CFR 1020 fluoroscopy regulations
Lower mAs capability, using the daily QC phantom measurement procedure
explanation: IR suites expose staff to significantly higher occupational doses due to prolonged fluoroscopy. Ceiling-mounted leaded glass shields, table-side drapes, lead aprons, thyroid shields, and dosimetry are essential protective measures.

q: The function of an anti-scatter grid in mammography differs from general radiography in that mammography grids are:
options: Not used in mammography, following ACR quality control protocols
Moving (reciprocating) grids with very fine grid ratios optimized for low kVp imaging
Stationary with high ratio, using the sensitometric quality control procedure
Made of aluminum instead of lead, per NCRP radiation protection guidelines
explanation: Mammography uses moving (reciprocating) grids to blur grid lines, with fine grid ratios (4:1 to 5:1) optimized for the low-energy X-ray beam used in mammography, improving contrast without unacceptable dose increase.

q: ACR accreditation for mammography requires phantom imaging to evaluate:
options: Only spatial resolution, as required by MQSA accreditation standards
Image quality including fibers, specks, and masses at minimum detectable levels
Dose only, following digital detector exposure index monitoring
Generator kVp accuracy only, per FDA 21 CFR 1020 fluoroscopy regulations
explanation: The ACR mammography phantom contains test objects simulating fibers, calcification specks, and tumor masses. Minimum pass criteria require visualization of specified numbers of each, ensuring adequate image quality for cancer detection.

q: MQSA (Mammography Quality Standards Act) requires mammography facilities to:
options: Only have FDA-approved equipment, as documented in the equipment performance baseline
Be accredited, have certified personnel, and undergo annual inspection
Report all exams to the FDA, per state radiation control program requirements
Use only digital equipment, using the sensitometric quality control procedure
explanation: MQSA requires all mammography facilities to be accredited by an FDA-approved body, employ certified radiologists/technologists/physicists, use certified equipment, and undergo annual MQSA inspections.

q: In computed radiography (CR), the image plate is read by:
options: A CCD camera directly, using the sensitometric quality control procedure
A laser beam that stimulates stored latent image energy as photostimulable luminescence
Direct X-ray exposure to a CCD, as measured by the calibrated dosimetry system
An amorphous selenium detector, per FDA 21 CFR 1020 fluoroscopy regulations
explanation: CR imaging plates contain photostimulable phosphor (BaFBr). After X-ray exposure, a scanning laser causes photostimulated luminescence (PSL) — light emission proportional to stored X-ray energy — which is collected and digitized.

q: The dynamic range of a digital X-ray detector compared to screen-film is:
options: Narrower, requiring precise exposure, using the sensitometric quality control procedure
Much wider, providing greater latitude and reducing retake rates from over/underexposure
Identical, per NRC occupational exposure regulations
Useful only at high kVp, using the daily QC phantom measurement procedure
explanation: Digital detectors have a much wider dynamic range than screen-film, capturing a broader range of exposures without loss of information. This reduces retakes from exposure errors but can mask poor technique if not monitored.

q: Exposure index (EI) in digital radiography is used to:
options: Set the kVp automatically, as documented in the equipment performance baseline
Indicate the amount of radiation that reached the detector, used for exposure monitoring and QC
Measure patient dose directly, using the daily QC phantom measurement procedure
Control grid ratio, per state radiation control program requirements
explanation: The Exposure Index (IEC 62494-1) is a standardized indicator of detector exposure level. Monitoring EI across exams helps technologists and physicists identify exposure creep and optimize technique factors.

q: In a DEXA (dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry) scan, bone mineral density is measured by:
options: Measuring CT Hounsfield units of bone, per IEC 62494-1 exposure index standardization
Using two X-ray energies to differentiate bone mineral from soft tissue by differential attenuation
Ultrasound reflection from bone surfaces, per NRC occupational exposure regulations
MRI T1 signal of bone marrow, following the manufacturer's kVp calibration procedure
explanation: DEXA uses two X-ray energies (typically 70 and 140 kVp equivalent). Since bone mineral and soft tissue attenuate the two energies differently, the system can calculate bone mineral density and T-score for osteoporosis assessment.

q: A strain gauge pressure transducer requires a Wheatstone bridge excitation voltage because:
options: It powers the display monitor, per 21 CFR Part 803 MDR reporting requirements
The bridge circuit converts small resistance changes to measurable voltage differences
It charges the backup battery, as required by EU MDR conformity assessment
It calibrates the zero offset automatically, as documented in the device master record
explanation: The excitation voltage powers the Wheatstone bridge. Pressure-induced resistance changes in the strain gauges unbalance the bridge, producing a differential output voltage proportional to pressure that can be amplified and displayed.

q: An electroencephalograph (EEG) records electrical activity of the brain with amplitude typically in the range of:
options: 1-10 Volts
1-100 millivolts
1-100 microvolts
1-10 nanovolts
explanation: EEG signals are extremely small — typically 1-100 uV (microvolts) — much smaller than ECG signals (millivolts). This requires very high gain amplifiers with excellent noise rejection and careful shielding.

q: Electromyography (EMG) measures:
options: Brain electrical activity, per ISO 13485 quality management requirements
Electrical activity produced by skeletal muscles
Heart electrical activity, as specified in the IEC 60601-1 safety standard
Nerve conduction velocity only, per 21 CFR Part 803 MDR reporting requirements
explanation: EMG records the electrical activity generated by skeletal muscle fibers during contraction, used to diagnose neuromuscular disorders, assess nerve damage, and guide injections or prosthetic control.

q: A pulse transit time (PTT) measurement can be used to estimate:
options: Heart rate only, per ISO 13485 quality management requirements
Blood pressure non-invasively by measuring the time between ECG R-wave and pulse arrival
SpO2 independently of pulse oximetry, per IEC 60601-2 particular requirements for the device type
Tidal volume, as required by EU MDR conformity assessment
explanation: PTT measures the time for the pulse wave to travel from the heart (ECG R-wave) to a peripheral site. Since PTT correlates with arterial stiffness and blood pressure, it can be used for cuffless blood pressure estimation.

q: In hospital telemetry systems, the central monitoring station receives patient ECG data via:
options: Direct cable from each patient room only, per 21 CFR Part 803 MDR reporting requirements
Wireless radio frequency (RF) transmission from bedside transmitters to a central receiver/server
Telephone landlines, following standard electrochemical measurement technique
Bluetooth only, as required by EU MDR conformity assessment
explanation: Cardiac telemetry uses RF wireless transmitters worn by patients that send ECG data to receivers/access points connected to a central monitoring station, allowing ambulatory patients to be monitored continuously.

q: The purpose of a crash cart (code cart) inspection by the BMET includes:
options: Restocking medications, as required by EU MDR conformity assessment
Verifying defibrillator readiness, testing AED/defibrillator function, and checking battery status
Training nurses on ACLS, per IEC 62304 medical device software lifecycle
Ordering replacement supplies, following ISO 14971 risk management procedures
explanation: BMET crash cart responsibilities include inspecting and testing the defibrillator/AED for proper function, charge capability, energy delivery accuracy, and battery condition — ensuring the device is ready for immediate emergency use.

q: Which standard governs the design and testing of software in medical devices?
explanation: IEC 62304 defines the software development lifecycle requirements for medical device software, including planning, requirements, design, implementation, testing, and maintenance — applied based on software safety classification.

q: Risk management for medical devices is governed by:
options: ISO 9001
IEC 60601-1 only
FDA 510k guidance only
explanation: ISO 14971 is the international standard for application of risk management to medical devices, covering hazard identification, risk estimation, evaluation, control, and residual risk assessment throughout the device lifecycle.

q: In biomedical signal processing, aliasing occurs when:
options: The signal amplitude exceeds the ADC range, per Spaulding classification infection control criteria
The sampling rate is less than twice the highest signal frequency (violating Nyquist theorem)
The filter cutoff is too high, per IEC 60601-2 particular requirements for the device type
The electrode impedance is too low, per IEC 62304 medical device software lifecycle
explanation: The Nyquist theorem requires sampling at least twice the highest signal frequency. Undersampling causes aliasing — high-frequency components are misrepresented as lower frequencies, corrupting the digitized signal.

q: A digital-to-analog converter (DAC) in a medical device is used to:
options: Convert patient data to HL7 messages, as verified by the quality assurance department
Convert digital numerical values to a corresponding analog voltage or current output
Compress image data, per IEC 60601-2 particular requirements for the device type
Filter noise from ECG signals, using the calibrated biomedical test equipment
explanation: A DAC converts digital binary values to proportional analog signals, used in applications like infusion pump motor control, physiologic waveform output ports, and audio alarm generation.

q: The difference between sterilization and disinfection is:
options: They are the same process, using validated sterilization cycle parameters
Sterilization destroys all microbial life including spores; disinfection reduces microbial load but may not eliminate spores
Disinfection is more thorough than sterilization, as specified in the IEC 60601-1 safety standard
Sterilization only applies to liquids, following validated reprocessing manufacturer guidelines
explanation: Sterilization eliminates all forms of microbial life including resistant bacterial spores. Disinfection destroys most pathogens but not necessarily spores, and is classified as high, intermediate, or low level based on efficacy.

q: High-level disinfection (HLD) is required for semicritical medical devices, which are items that:
options: Contact only intact skin, following CAPA root cause analysis procedures
Contact mucous membranes or non-intact skin but do not penetrate sterile tissue
Are implanted surgically, per ISO 13485 quality management requirements
Never contact patients, as specified in the IEC 60601-1 safety standard
explanation: Per Spaulding classification, semicritical devices (endoscopes, laryngoscope blades, respiratory therapy equipment) contact mucous membranes and require at minimum high-level disinfection to eliminate all microorganisms except some bacterial spores.

q: Endoscope reprocessing between patients must include as a minimum:
options: Rinsing with water only, following validated reprocessing manufacturer guidelines
Pre-cleaning, leak testing, manual cleaning, high-level disinfection or sterilization, rinsing, drying, and storage
Autoclave sterilization only, as required by EU MDR conformity assessment
UV light disinfection only, per 21 CFR Part 803 MDR reporting requirements
explanation: Flexible endoscope reprocessing requires: bedside pre-clean, leak test, manual cleaning in enzymatic detergent, automated or manual high-level disinfection (e.g., glutaraldehyde, OPA, peracetic acid), thorough rinsing, forced-air drying, and proper storage.

q: Which type of pacemaker lead placement is considered permanent and requires fluoroscopic guidance?
options: Transcutaneous
Transvenous endocardial
Transthoracic epicardial (external only)
Esophageal
explanation: Permanent transvenous pacemakers are implanted by threading leads through a vein into the right heart chambers under fluoroscopic guidance. Temporary transcutaneous pacing uses external pads for emergency use.

q: The primary difference between an AED and a manual defibrillator is:
options: AEDs deliver more energy, following OSHA lockout/tagout requirements
AEDs automatically analyze rhythm and advise shock without operator rhythm interpretation
Manual defibrillators are battery-powered only, per The Joint Commission equipment management standards
AEDs can only be used by physicians, using an appropriate biomedical test instrument
explanation: An AED uses built-in algorithms to analyze the patient's rhythm and determines if a shockable rhythm (VF or pulseless VT) is present, then prompts the operator to deliver a shock — no rhythm interpretation skills required.

q: A biphasic defibrillator waveform is preferred over monophasic because it:
options: Delivers more total energy, following standard biomed department protocols
Is equally effective at lower energy levels, reducing myocardial damage
Is simpler to manufacture, according to AAMI ES1 safety requirements
Works without a return electrode, as required by IEC 60601-1 standards
explanation: Biphasic waveforms reverse current direction mid-shock, improving defibrillation efficacy at lower energy (120-200 J vs 360 J monophasic), reducing post-shock myocardial dysfunction and skin burns.

q: Invasive arterial blood pressure monitoring requires zeroing the transducer to:
options: Calibrate the display color, per FDA 21 CFR Part 820 design control requirements
Eliminate the effect of atmospheric pressure and hydrostatic pressure from the reading
Test the alarm limits, per The Joint Commission equipment management standards
Set the systolic threshold, using an appropriate biomedical test instrument
explanation: Zeroing opens the transducer to atmosphere at the phlebostatic axis (right atrium level), setting the baseline reference to zero so that only intravascular pressure is measured, eliminating atmospheric and column pressure effects.

q: The phlebostatic axis used for zeroing hemodynamic transducers is located at:
options: The femoral artery, per The Joint Commission equipment management standards
The fourth intercostal space, midaxillary line — approximate level of the right atrium
The antecubital fossa, per FDA 21 CFR Part 820 design control requirements
The top of the patient's head, following standard biomed department protocols
explanation: The phlebostatic axis (4th ICS, midaxillary line) approximates the level of the right atrium. Zeroing and leveling at this point ensures accurate hemodynamic pressure measurements regardless of patient height.

q: Central venous pressure (CVP) monitoring reflects:
options: Left ventricular function, after verifying proper ground continuity
Right atrial pressure — an indicator of right heart function and venous return
Pulmonary artery pressure, following standard biomed department protocols
Systemic arterial pressure, as required by IEC 60601-1 standards
explanation: CVP reflects right atrial filling pressure, providing information about right heart function, circulating blood volume, and venous return. Normal CVP is 2-8 mmHg.

q: A pulse pressure is calculated as:
options: Diastolic minus systolic pressure
Systolic minus diastolic pressure
Mean arterial pressure minus CVP
Systolic pressure divided by heart rate
explanation: Pulse pressure = systolic BP - diastolic BP. Normal is approximately 40 mmHg. Wide pulse pressure can indicate aortic regurgitation or increased stroke volume; narrow pulse pressure may indicate cardiogenic shock.

q: Mean arterial pressure (MAP) is most accurately calculated as:
options: (Systolic + Diastolic) / 2, following standard biomed department protocols
Diastolic + 1/3 (Systolic - Diastolic)
Systolic x 0.75, as specified in the preventive maintenance schedule
Diastolic x 2 / Systolic, using the manufacturer-specified calibration procedure
explanation: MAP = DBP + 1/3(PP) or DBP + 1/3(SBP - DBP). Since diastole is approximately twice as long as systole, diastole is weighted more heavily. Normal MAP is 70-100 mmHg.

q: Hemodialysis equipment functions by:
options: Filtering blood through the lungs artificially, according to AAMI ES1 safety requirements
Passing blood through a semipermeable membrane to remove waste products and excess fluid via diffusion and ultrafiltration
Oxygenating blood outside the body, as required by IEC 60601-1 standards
Using UV light to sterilize blood, per NFPA 99 patient-care area guidelines
explanation: Hemodialysis circulates blood through a dialyzer (artificial kidney) where waste products and excess electrolytes diffuse across a semipermeable membrane into dialysate, and excess fluid is removed by ultrafiltration (pressure gradient).

q: A blood warmer used during rapid transfusion is checked during PM for:
options: Flow rate accuracy only, as required by IEC 60601-1 standards
Temperature accuracy, high-temperature alarm function, and pressure limits
Filter integrity only, following OSHA lockout/tagout requirements
Electrical output waveform, per The Joint Commission equipment management standards
explanation: Blood warmer PM verifies that the device heats to the target temperature accurately (typically 37-42°C), that the high-temp alarm activates to prevent hemolysis, and that pressure limits protecting the blood bag are functioning.

q: Which type of fire is most likely in an oxygen-enriched environment such as an OR?
options: Electrical fire only, verified with a calibrated patient safety analyzer
Accelerated combustion fire — ignition sources cause fires that burn more intensely in elevated O2
Chemical fire, per FDA 21 CFR Part 820 design control requirements
Smoldering fire only, as required by IEC 60601-1 standards
explanation: Oxygen-enriched environments (>21% O2) dramatically accelerate combustion. In ORs, the combination of surgical drapes, ignition sources (ESUs, lasers), and supplemental O2 creates significant surgical fire risk.

q: The resistance-capacitance (RC) time constant in an electronic circuit represents:
options: The frequency at resonance, per FDA 21 CFR Part 820 design control requirements
The time for the capacitor to charge to approximately 63% of the supply voltage
The power dissipated in the resistor, after verifying proper ground continuity
The impedance at DC, following standard biomed department protocols
explanation: The RC time constant (tau = R x C) is the time for a capacitor to charge to 63.2% (1 - 1/e) of the applied voltage through a resistor, or discharge to 36.8% of its initial value. It determines the response time of RC circuits.

q: In a transformer, the turns ratio determines:
options: Core material properties, verified with a calibrated patient safety analyzer
The ratio of primary to secondary voltage (and inverse ratio of currents)
The frequency response, using the manufacturer-specified calibration procedure
The power factor, according to AAMI ES1 safety requirements
explanation: Transformer turns ratio (N1/N2) = V1/V2 = I2/I1. A step-down transformer with a 10:1 turns ratio reduces voltage by 10x while increasing current by 10x (assuming ideal transformer, ignoring losses).

q: Which type of ADC is most commonly used in high-speed medical imaging applications?
options: Dual-slope integrating ADC
Successive approximation ADC (SAR)
Flash ADC
Sigma-delta ADC
explanation: Flash ADCs use a parallel bank of comparators to convert all bits simultaneously, making them the fastest ADC type — ideal for high-speed imaging, ultrasound beamforming, and CT detector readout applications.

q: Power factor in an AC circuit is defined as:
options: Apparent power divided by reactive power
Real (true) power divided by apparent power
Voltage divided by current
Impedance divided by resistance
explanation: Power factor (PF) = Real Power (W) / Apparent Power (VA) = cos(theta). A PF of 1.0 means all power is doing useful work. Capacitive/inductive loads reduce PF, wasting energy in reactive components.

q: In a hospital medical gas system, the color of a nitrous oxide pipeline is:
options: Green
Blue
Yellow
Gray
explanation: NFPA 99 and CGA standards designate blue for nitrous oxide pipelines and cylinders in the US. Oxygen is green, medical air is yellow, nitrogen is black, and carbon dioxide is gray.

q: The DISS (Diameter Index Safety System) on medical gas connections prevents:
options: Pressure surges in pipelines, following OSHA lockout/tagout requirements
Incorrect gas connections by using unique non-interchangeable fittings for each gas
Contamination of gas supply, per NFPA 99 patient-care area guidelines
Flow rate fluctuations, per FDA 21 CFR Part 820 design control requirements
explanation: DISS fittings have unique diameter combinations for each medical gas, making it physically impossible to connect the wrong gas to equipment — a critical safety feature preventing potentially fatal gas mix-ups.

q: A hospital vacuum system is used for:
options: Delivering anesthetic gases, as documented in the service manual protocol
Suctioning fluids and gases from patients and waste anesthetic gas scavenging
Pressurizing OR rooms, per FDA 21 CFR Part 820 design control requirements
Cooling MRI magnets, in compliance with hospital electrical safety policy
explanation: Medical vacuum provides suction for patient airway suctioning, surgical field evacuation, chest drainage, and waste anesthetic gas scavenging (WAGD) systems, removing waste gases from the OR environment.

q: Pin Index Safety System (PISS) on gas cylinders prevents:
options: Cylinder overpressure, using the manufacturer-specified calibration procedure
Wrong gas cylinder being connected to anesthesia equipment by using unique pin configurations per gas
Regulator failure, using an appropriate biomedical test instrument
Cylinder rolling hazards, per NFPA 99 patient-care area guidelines
explanation: PISS uses a specific two-pin pattern unique to each gas type on the yoke connection of small cylinders (E-size), preventing inadvertent connection of the wrong gas cylinder to anesthesia machines or other equipment.

q: Photoplethysmography (PPG) is the sensing principle used in:
options: Invasive arterial lines, as indicated by the leakage current measurement
Pulse oximeters and some wearable heart rate monitors
ECG machines, following OSHA lockout/tagout requirements
Capnographs, verified with a calibrated patient safety analyzer
explanation: PPG detects volumetric changes in blood vessels by measuring variations in light absorption/reflection through tissue. Pulse oximeters use PPG to detect the pulse waveform and calculate SpO2 and heart rate.

q: A sequential compression device (SCD) used for DVT prophylaxis works by:
options: Delivering electrical stimulation to leg muscles, as required by IEC 60601-1 standards
Cyclically inflating pneumatic sleeves to compress the legs and promote venous return
Cooling the legs to reduce clot formation, as specified in the preventive maintenance schedule
Vibrating the legs at ultrasonic frequencies, in compliance with hospital electrical safety policy
explanation: SCDs intermittently inflate air bladders in leg sleeves from distal to proximal, mimicking the calf muscle pump to augment venous return, reducing venous stasis and DVT risk in immobile patients.

q: The primary safety concern when servicing laser medical equipment is:
options: Electric shock only, per FDA 21 CFR Part 820 design control requirements
Eye and skin injury from direct or reflected laser beam exposure
RF interference, following standard biomed department protocols
Magnetic field exposure, per The Joint Commission equipment management standards
explanation: Medical lasers pose serious hazards including permanent eye injury (even from reflected beams) and skin burns. Appropriate laser safety eyewear matched to the specific laser wavelength is mandatory, along with controlled access and beam stop protocols.

q: Which standard classifies lasers by hazard level?
options: NFPA 99
ANSI Z136.1
explanation: ANSI Z136.1 is the American National Standard for the Safe Use of Lasers, which classifies lasers into classes (1, 1M, 2, 2M, 3R, 3B, 4) based on output power and potential for injury, defining required controls for each class.

q: Ultrasonic surgical aspirators (CUSA) used in neurosurgery work by:
options: Using RF energy to cut tissue, as required by IEC 60601-1 standards
Using ultrasonic vibration to emulsify and aspirate soft tissue while sparing blood vessels and nerves
Freezing tissue with cryogenic gas, per NFPA 99 patient-care area guidelines
Using a high-pressure water jet, after verifying proper ground continuity
explanation: Cavitational Ultrasonic Surgical Aspirators vibrate a titanium tip at ultrasonic frequencies, selectively emulsifying high-water-content tissue (tumors) via cavitation while sparing fibrous blood vessels and nerve tissue.

q: A hospital bed's nurse call and code blue functions are tested during PM to verify:
options: Mattress firmness settings, per NFPA 99 patient-care area guidelines
That call signals reach the nursing station and code functions activate the appropriate emergency response
Bed weight capacity, as indicated by the leakage current measurement
Motor current draw only, as documented in the service manual protocol
explanation: Hospital bed PM includes testing all electrical functions: nurse call integration, bed exit alarms, code blue button activation, and communication with the nurse call system — critical for patient safety and emergency response.

q: In a CT scanner, the bow-tie filter is used to:
options: Remove low-energy photons to harden the beam, per FDA 21 CFR 1020 fluoroscopy regulations
Shape the beam profile to match patient anatomy, reducing dose at the periphery where the patient is thinner
Collimate the beam to the selected slice thickness, per NRC occupational exposure regulations
Filter scatter from the detector, in accordance with ALARA dose optimization principles
explanation: The bow-tie (beam-shaping) filter is thicker at the edges and thinner at the center, attenuating the beam more at the periphery where patients are thinner — equalizing detector exposure across the field and reducing skin dose.

q: Dual-energy CT (DECT) acquires data at two different kVp settings to:
options: Reduce scan time by half, per IEC 62494-1 exposure index standardization
Differentiate materials by their energy-dependent attenuation, enabling virtual non-contrast, material decomposition, and uric acid characterization
Improve temporal resolution for cardiac imaging, as measured by the calibrated dosimetry system
Double the detector coverage, following digital detector exposure index monitoring
explanation: DECT exploits the different X-ray attenuation characteristics of materials at two energies. Applications include characterizing renal stones (uric acid vs. calcium), virtual non-contrast images, iodine maps, and virtual monoenergetic images.

q: Gantry tilt in CT is used to:
options: Increase patient throughput, following the manufacturer's kVp calibration procedure
Angle the scan plane to avoid dose-sensitive structures or align with anatomy
Reduce image noise, as documented in the equipment performance baseline
Change the field of view, in accordance with ALARA dose optimization principles
explanation: Gantry tilt angles the scan plane relative to the patient axis, used to align with spinal anatomy, reduce beam-hardening artifacts from dental fillings, and decrease dose to radiosensitive structures like the lens of the eye.

q: The purpose of automatic tube current modulation (ATCM) in CT is to:
options: Keep image noise constant by varying mA based on patient attenuation, reducing dose in less attenuating regions
Automatically select kVp, per state radiation control program requirements
Control gantry rotation speed, using the sensitometric quality control procedure
Adjust detector row selection, following ACR quality control protocols
explanation: ATCM modulates tube current (mA) in real-time based on patient size and attenuation from scout images or real-time feedback. It reduces dose in thinner body regions while maintaining adequate image quality in denser areas.

q: In fluoroscopy, pulsed fluoroscopy reduces patient dose by:
options: Using lower kVp, using the sensitometric quality control procedure
Delivering X-rays in discrete pulses rather than continuously, reducing total exposure time
Using a smaller detector, per state radiation control program requirements
Increasing SID, as required by MQSA accreditation standards
explanation: Pulsed fluoroscopy delivers X-rays only during brief pulses (e.g., 7.5, 15, or 30 pulses/second) rather than continuously. Last image hold and road-mapping features further reduce dose by eliminating unnecessary continuous fluoroscopy.

q: Radiation dose to the embryo/fetus during medical imaging is most critical during:
options: Third trimester only, per NCRP radiation protection guidelines
First trimester — particularly organogenesis (weeks 2-8)
Delivery only, as documented in the equipment performance baseline
All trimesters equally, as measured by the calibrated dosimetry system
explanation: The embryo/fetus is most radiosensitive during organogenesis (weeks 2-8), when radiation can cause structural malformations. Risks include growth restriction, CNS effects (8-15 weeks), and increased childhood cancer risk at higher doses.

q: The occupational whole-body dose limit per year for radiation workers in the US (NRC) is:
options: 1 mSv (100 mrem)
5 mSv (500 mrem)
50 mSv (5 rem)
100 mSv (10 rem)
explanation: The NRC annual occupational whole-body effective dose limit is 50 mSv (5 rem) per year. The lens of the eye limit is 150 mSv and extremity limit is 500 mSv. The ALARA principle requires doses be kept well below these limits.

q: Thermoluminescent dosimeters (TLDs) measure radiation dose by:
options: Chemical color change in a film, following the manufacturer's kVp calibration procedure
Storing radiation energy in a crystal lattice released as light when heated
Ionization of gas in a sealed chamber, following digital detector exposure index monitoring
Photostimulated luminescence, in accordance with ALARA dose optimization principles
explanation: TLDs (typically LiF crystals) store radiation-induced trapped electrons in crystal defects. When heated, electrons return to ground state emitting light proportional to absorbed dose, which is measured by a photomultiplier.

q: In mammography, the average glandular dose (AGD) is the preferred dose metric because:
options: It is easiest to measure directly, per NRC occupational exposure regulations
Glandular tissue is most radiosensitive in the breast and most relevant for radiation risk assessment
It equals entrance skin dose, using the sensitometric quality control procedure
It is required by MQSA for billing, as required by MQSA accreditation standards
explanation: AGD is preferred over entrance skin exposure because glandular tissue carries the primary radiation risk for breast cancer induction. AGD is calculated from entrance exposure, kVp, HVL, and breast composition/thickness.

q: Tomosynthesis in mammography improves cancer detection by:
options: Using higher kVp for better penetration, per NRC occupational exposure regulations
Acquiring multiple low-dose projections to reconstruct 3D slices, reducing overlapping tissue obscuring lesions
Using contrast media injection, as required by MQSA accreditation standards
Doubling the compression force, as measured by the calibrated dosimetry system
explanation: Digital breast tomosynthesis (DBT) acquires multiple low-dose images at different angles and reconstructs thin slices through the breast, reducing the masking effect of overlapping glandular tissue that can hide cancers or create false positives on 2D mammography.

q: In MRI, chemical shift artifact occurs because:
options: Patient motion during scanning, following the vendor-specific image reconstruction parameters
Fat and water protons precess at slightly different frequencies, causing spatial misregistration in the frequency-encode direction
Metallic implants distort the field, per NRC decay-in-storage radioactive waste requirements
Gradient coils overheat, as specified in the MRI site planning documentation
explanation: Fat protons resonate at ~3.5 ppm lower frequency than water. The scanner interprets this frequency difference as a spatial shift, displacing fat signal relative to water signal in the frequency-encode direction — visible at fat-water interfaces.

q: Magnetic susceptibility artifacts in MRI are most severe near:
options: Fat-water interfaces, as specified by the DICOM conformance statement
Metallic implants, surgical clips, or air-tissue interfaces
Large blood vessels, per HL7 FHIR RESTful API interoperability standards
Bone-muscle interfaces, following DICOM GSDF display calibration protocol
explanation: Susceptibility artifacts arise from local magnetic field distortions at interfaces between materials with very different magnetic susceptibilities — most severe near ferromagnetic metals, causing signal loss, geometric distortion, and blooming artifacts.

q: The purpose of fat suppression techniques in MRI is to:
options: Reduce scan time, following the vendor-specific image reconstruction parameters
Null the fat signal to improve visualization of adjacent structures or detect bone marrow edema
Increase SNR in all tissues, as specified in the MRI site planning documentation
Reduce motion artifact, as verified by the qualified medical physicist
explanation: Fat suppression (STIR, spectral fat sat, Dixon) eliminates the bright fat signal that can obscure adjacent pathology, improve contrast after gadolinium injection, and detect bone marrow edema — where fat is replaced by water.

q: Gadolinium contrast agents in MRI work by:
options: Directly producing signal on T1 images, as verified by the qualified medical physicist
Shortening T1 relaxation time of nearby protons, causing T1 brightening in enhancing tissues
Blocking T2 signal in vessels, as specified by the DICOM conformance statement
Absorbing radiofrequency pulses, following standard MR conditional implant screening protocol
explanation: Gadolinium is a paramagnetic metal that shortens T1 (and T2) relaxation times of nearby water protons. On T1-weighted images, tissues with gadolinium accumulation appear bright, highlighting areas of blood-brain barrier breakdown or hypervascularity.

q: In ultrasound, posterior acoustic enhancement occurs distal to:
options: Highly reflective structures like bone, as measured by the calibrated dosimetry phantom
Fluid-filled structures (cysts, vessels) that attenuate sound less than surrounding tissue
Dense calcifications, as specified in the MRI site planning documentation
Air-filled structures, per DRL diagnostic reference level benchmarks
explanation: Fluid transmits sound with less attenuation than soft tissue. Structures distal to a cyst or fluid collection receive more sound energy than expected, appearing brighter than surrounding tissue — posterior acoustic enhancement.

q: The Doppler equation used in ultrasound to calculate flow velocity is:
options: v = f x wavelength, per DRL diagnostic reference level benchmarks
v = (c x delta-f) / (2 x f0 x cos theta)
v = amplitude / time, as measured by the calibrated dosimetry phantom
v = pressure / resistance, per HL7 FHIR RESTful API interoperability standards
explanation: The Doppler equation: v = (c x Δf) / (2 x f0 x cos θ), where c = speed of sound, Δf = Doppler frequency shift, f0 = transmit frequency, θ = angle between beam and flow. Angle correction is critical — angles >60° cause significant error.

q: In nuclear medicine, a collimator on a gamma camera is used to:
options: Focus gamma rays like an optical lens, per HL7 FHIR RESTful API interoperability standards
Select only gamma rays traveling in specific directions, providing spatial information for image formation
Filter out low-energy gamma rays, as specified by the DICOM conformance statement
Amplify the gamma ray signal, as verified by the qualified medical physicist
explanation: Gamma rays cannot be focused like light. The lead collimator uses geometry — only allowing gamma rays traveling nearly parallel to its holes to reach the crystal — providing spatial discrimination and enabling image formation.

q: Radioactive waste disposal in a nuclear medicine department is governed by:
options: EPA only, per NRC decay-in-storage radioactive waste requirements
NRC (Nuclear Regulatory Commission) regulations — typically decay in storage for short-lived isotopes
OSHA regulations only, per NRC 10 CFR Part 35 medical use of radioactive material
Hospital discretion, as documented in the PACS system configuration
explanation: NRC regulations govern radioactive waste. Most nuclear medicine waste uses short-lived isotopes (e.g., Tc-99m T½ = 6 hrs) and is managed by decay-in-storage — held until activity drops to background levels before disposal as regular waste.

q: Image stitching in long-length radiography (e.g., full spine/leg) is performed to:
options: Reduce radiation dose, following the manufacturer's kVp calibration procedure
Combine multiple overlapping images into a single seamless image of an anatomy longer than the detector
Improve contrast in extremities, as measured by the calibrated dosimetry system
Enable 3D reconstruction, following ACR quality control protocols
explanation: Full-length studies (scoliosis, leg length discrepancy) require multiple detector-length acquisitions that are digitally stitched using anatomical landmarks into a single image, enabling measurement of alignment, curves, and lengths.

q: The function of a voltage regulator integrated circuit (e.g., 7805) in a medical device power supply is to:
options: Convert AC to DC, per ISO 13485 quality management requirements
Maintain a stable fixed output voltage despite variations in input voltage or load current
Store energy like a capacitor, using validated sterilization cycle parameters
Step up voltage, following validated reprocessing manufacturer guidelines
explanation: Linear voltage regulators (78xx series) maintain a fixed output voltage by dissipating excess voltage as heat. The 7805 provides a stable 5V output, essential for powering microcontrollers and digital circuits in medical devices.

q: In a switching power supply used in medical equipment, switching frequency is typically in the range of:
options: 50-60 Hz, per ISO 13485 quality management requirements
1-10 kHz, per IEC 60601-2 particular requirements for the device type
20 kHz - 2 MHz
Above 100 MHz
explanation: Switching power supplies operate at 20 kHz to 2 MHz, far above audio range. Higher switching frequencies allow smaller transformers and filter components, improving power density and efficiency compared to linear supplies.

q: A hospital UPS (Uninterruptible Power Supply) for critical medical equipment provides:
options: Increased voltage during normal operation, as documented in the device master record
Seamless battery backup power during main power failure, preventing equipment shutdown
Surge protection only, per IEC 60601-2 particular requirements for the device type
Power factor correction only, as specified in the IEC 60601-1 safety standard
explanation: A UPS continuously monitors incoming power and provides instantaneous battery backup (online UPS) or near-instantaneous switchover (offline/line-interactive) during outages, protecting critical equipment from power interruptions.

q: Type I versus Type II errors in medical device testing refer to:
options: Hardware versus software failures, following ISO 14971 risk management procedures
False positive (rejecting a good device) versus false negative (accepting a defective device) errors
Analog versus digital measurement errors, using validated sterilization cycle parameters
Systematic versus random errors, per 21 CFR Part 803 MDR reporting requirements
explanation: In device testing, a Type I error (false positive/alpha error) incorrectly rejects a conforming device; a Type II error (false negative/beta error) incorrectly accepts a non-conforming device — the latter is more dangerous in medical devices.

q: Design validation for a medical device per FDA design controls verifies that:
options: The device meets design specifications, per 21 CFR Part 803 MDR reporting requirements
The device meets user needs and intended use in actual or simulated use conditions
Manufacturing processes are consistent, following validated reprocessing manufacturer guidelines
Software code is defect-free, as required by EU MDR conformity assessment
explanation: Validation answers 'did we build the right device?' — confirming the device meets user needs in real-world use. Verification answers 'did we build it correctly?' — confirming specifications are met. Both are required per 21 CFR 820 design controls.

q: Corrective and Preventive Action (CAPA) in a medical device quality system is triggered by:
options: Routine PM only, as verified by the quality assurance department
Identified nonconformities, complaints, audit findings, or adverse events to prevent recurrence
Annual management reviews only, per IEC 60601-2 particular requirements for the device type
New product launches, per IEC 62304 medical device software lifecycle
explanation: CAPA (21 CFR 820.100, ISO 13485) is a systematic process to identify root causes of problems, implement corrections (fix the current issue) and preventive actions (prevent recurrence), and verify effectiveness.

q: The primary difference between a Class IIa and Class IIb medical device under EU MDR is:
options: IIa is active, IIb is passive, as verified by the quality assurance department
IIb poses higher risk and requires a notified body for conformity assessment vs IIa which may use technical documentation only
IIa requires sterilization, IIb does not, per IEC 62304 medical device software lifecycle
IIb is implantable only, as required by EU MDR conformity assessment
explanation: Under EU MDR, Class IIa (medium risk) and IIb (medium-high risk) both require notified body involvement, but IIb requires examination of the technical documentation and design dossier — a more rigorous review than IIa.

q: Unique Device Identification (UDI) required by FDA on medical devices allows:
options: Wireless tracking of devices in hospitals only, as required by EU MDR conformity assessment
Rapid and accurate identification of devices in adverse event reporting, recalls, and inventory management
Automatic billing to insurance, as documented in the device master record
Remote software updates, following CAPA root cause analysis procedures
explanation: FDA UDI requirements mandate a standardized identifier (barcode/RFID) on device labels, enabling traceability from manufacturer to patient, facilitating recalls, adverse event investigation, and supply chain management in hospital CMMS systems.

q: Which nerve is most commonly injured during improper patient positioning on an OR table?
options: Femoral nerve
Ulnar nerve
Brachial plexus
Sciatic nerve
explanation: The brachial plexus is the most commonly injured nerve during surgical positioning, particularly with arm abduction >90 degrees, excessive lateral head rotation, or steep Trendelenburg position — causing postoperative neuropathy.

q: Which standard governs the safe design of alarm systems on medical devices?
options: ISO 14971, verified with a calibrated patient safety analyzer
NFPA 99, per FDA 21 CFR Part 820 design control requirements
explanation: IEC 60601-1-8 is the collateral standard specifically governing alarm systems in medical electrical equipment, defining alarm signal characteristics, priorities (high/medium/low), and alarm management requirements.

q: Alarm fatigue in clinical settings refers to:
options: Alarms that are too quiet to hear, as required by IEC 60601-1 standards
Desensitization of clinical staff due to excessive alarm frequency, leading to missed critical alarms
Equipment alarms that drain batteries, using the manufacturer-specified calibration procedure
Alarms that interfere with patient sleep, as specified in the preventive maintenance schedule
explanation: Alarm fatigue occurs when clinicians become desensitized to the high volume of alarms (many false or non-actionable), causing delayed response or alarm silencing — a significant patient safety issue addressed by TJC National Patient Safety Goals.

q: The purpose of a watchdog timer circuit in a medical device microcontroller is to:
options: Keep time and date accurate, according to AAMI ES1 safety requirements
Automatically reset the processor if software becomes unresponsive, preventing device lockup
Monitor battery voltage, in compliance with hospital electrical safety policy
Control display brightness, using an appropriate biomedical test instrument
explanation: A watchdog timer is a hardware counter that must be periodically reset by running software. If the software crashes or hangs, the timer expires and forces a system reset — a critical safety mechanism in life-sustaining devices.

q: Electromagnetic compatibility (EMC) testing of medical devices evaluates:
options: Battery life under load, verified with a calibrated patient safety analyzer
The device's ability to function in its electromagnetic environment without causing or suffering interference
Network connectivity, using the manufacturer-specified calibration procedure
Thermal performance only, per NFPA 99 patient-care area guidelines
explanation: EMC testing per IEC 60601-1-2 verifies both emissions (device doesn't interfere with others) and immunity (device functions correctly when exposed to RF fields, ESD, conducted disturbances, and power line transients).

q: Which type of memory is used to store firmware in most modern medical devices?
options: DRAM, in compliance with hospital electrical safety policy
SRAM, following OSHA lockout/tagout requirements
Flash (EEPROM)
Magnetic core memory
explanation: Flash memory (a type of EEPROM) stores firmware non-volatilely and can be updated in-circuit, making it ideal for medical device firmware. It retains data without power and allows field software updates.

q: A medical device's essential performance is defined as:
options: All features listed in the user manual, per FDA 21 CFR Part 820 design control requirements
Performance necessary to avoid unacceptable risk — loss of which creates patient hazard beyond basic safety
Maximum output capability, following standard biomed department protocols
CE marking requirements, as documented in the service manual protocol
explanation: Per IEC 60601-1, essential performance is the clinical function(s) whose loss or degradation results in unacceptable risk. Devices must maintain essential performance under single fault conditions and specified environmental conditions.

q: The Penlon test (or similar respiratory circuit leak test) on an anesthesia machine verifies:
options: Vaporizer calibration, following OSHA lockout/tagout requirements
That the breathing circuit and machine are leak-free before use on a patient
Gas scavenging flow rate, verified with a calibrated patient safety analyzer
Ventilator tidal volume accuracy, in compliance with hospital electrical safety policy
explanation: Pre-use anesthesia machine checkout includes a high-pressure and low-pressure leak test to ensure the breathing circuit, one-way valves, CO2 absorber, and vaporizer connections are gas-tight, preventing anesthetic agent leaks.

q: Carbon dioxide absorbers in anesthesia machines (e.g., soda lime) function to:
options: Add CO2 to the breathing circuit, as documented in the service manual protocol
Chemically absorb exhaled CO2 to allow rebreathing of anesthetic gases in a circle system
Monitor CO2 concentration, per The Joint Commission equipment management standards
Scavenge waste gases, after verifying proper ground continuity
explanation: Soda lime (NaOH/Ca(OH)2) absorbs CO2 via an exothermic chemical reaction in the circle breathing system, allowing rebreathing of exhaled gas while preventing CO2 accumulation — reducing anesthetic agent consumption.

q: Which parameter is monitored by a capnograph waveform shape to detect esophageal intubation?
options: Elevated plateau CO2, using an appropriate biomedical test instrument
Absent CO2 waveform or rapidly declining trace after initial puffs
Elevated respiratory rate, according to AAMI ES1 safety requirements
Prolonged inspiratory phase, verified with a calibrated patient safety analyzer
explanation: Esophageal intubation produces no sustained CO2 waveform (or a few decreasing puffs of gastric CO2 then flat line), while correct tracheal placement produces consistent rectangular waveforms with EtCO2 of 35-45 mmHg.

q: The purpose of a high-flow nasal cannula (HFNC) system is to:
options: Deliver standard 2-6 L/min O2 like a regular cannula, according to AAMI ES1 safety requirements
Deliver heated humidified oxygen at flows up to 60 L/min, providing CPAP-like pressure and improved oxygenation
Replace mechanical ventilation in all patients, as documented in the service manual protocol
Deliver nebulized medications only, per The Joint Commission equipment management standards
explanation: HFNC delivers heated, humidified blended oxygen at high flow rates (20-60 L/min), flushing nasopharyngeal dead space, generating low-level CPAP (1-2 cmH2O per 10 L/min), and improving oxygenation in hypoxemic respiratory failure.

q: A flow sensor in a mechanical ventilator most commonly uses which measurement principle?
options: Ultrasonic Doppler, using the manufacturer-specified calibration procedure
Differential pressure across a known resistance (pneumotachograph) or hot-wire anemometry
Turbine rotation speed, verified with a calibrated patient safety analyzer
Optical fiber bending, using an appropriate biomedical test instrument
explanation: Pneumotachographs measure the pressure differential across a known resistance (screen or tube bundle) using the linear relationship between flow and pressure drop at low Reynolds numbers. Hot-wire anemometers measure cooling of a heated wire by gas flow.

q: During a ventilator PM, pressure accuracy is verified by comparing the ventilator's displayed pressure to:
options: A water manometer only, after verifying proper ground continuity
A calibrated reference pressure gauge or ventilator tester simultaneously measuring circuit pressure
The manufacturer's stated accuracy, as indicated by the leakage current measurement
A separate pulse oximeter reading, per NFPA 99 patient-care area guidelines
explanation: Ventilator PM requires connecting a calibrated reference analyzer (e.g., Fluke VT Plus, IMT CITREX) to simultaneously measure pressure, flow, and volume while comparing to the ventilator's displayed values to verify accuracy within specifications.

q: Ohm's acoustic law (analogous to Ohm's electrical law) relates flow, pressure, and resistance in ventilator circuits as:
options: Flow = Pressure x Resistance
Pressure = Flow x Resistance
Resistance = Flow + Pressure
Flow = Resistance / Pressure
explanation: Analogous to V = IR in electrical circuits, airway pressure = flow x resistance in respiratory mechanics. This relationship is fundamental to understanding ventilator pressure-flow-resistance relationships and troubleshooting patient-ventilator dyssynchrony.

q: The purpose of spirometry testing in pulmonary function testing is to:
options: Measure oxygen saturation, in compliance with hospital electrical safety policy
Measure lung volumes and airflow rates to assess respiratory function and diagnose obstructive/restrictive disease
Monitor continuous breathing during sleep, as indicated by the leakage current measurement
Measure arterial blood gas values, using an appropriate biomedical test instrument
explanation: Spirometry measures FVC (forced vital capacity), FEV1 (forced expiratory volume in 1 second), FEV1/FVC ratio, and flow-volume curves to differentiate obstructive (low FEV1/FVC, e.g., COPD, asthma) from restrictive (low FVC, normal ratio) patterns.

q: In a cardiac catheterization lab, the hemodynamic recording system must have a frequency response of at least:
options: 1-5 Hz, per NFPA 99 patient-care area guidelines
0-10 Hz, following standard biomed department protocols
0-50 Hz or higher
100-200 Hz only
explanation: Accurate hemodynamic waveform recording requires a frequency response of at least 0-50 Hz (ideally higher) to faithfully reproduce the high-frequency components of arterial and ventricular pressure waveforms without distortion.

q: Static electricity buildup in an OR is mitigated by:
options: Increasing humidity above 70%, following OSHA lockout/tagout requirements
Maintaining relative humidity at 20-60% and using conductive/antistatic flooring and footwear
Using only cotton materials, using an appropriate biomedical test instrument
Grounding all staff directly to earth, verified with a calibrated patient safety analyzer
explanation: NFPA 99 recommends OR humidity of 20-60% (historically higher) to reduce static buildup. Conductive flooring dissipates static charges safely. This is critical historically with flammable anesthetics and remains relevant for ESD-sensitive electronics.

q: A surgical robot (e.g., da Vinci) biomed inspection typically includes:
options: Only software version check, in compliance with hospital electrical safety policy
Mechanical arm calibration, instrument tracking verification, video system check, and electrical safety testing
Patient positioning only, per NFPA 99 patient-care area guidelines
Sterilization validation only, after verifying proper ground continuity
explanation: Surgical robot PM is complex and typically manufacturer-guided, including mechanical calibration of arms and instruments, verification of tremor filtering and motion scaling, camera/optics inspection, electrical safety testing, and software/firmware verification.

q: The output impedance of a medical device signal output should ideally be:
options: Very high to maximize voltage, as specified in the preventive maintenance schedule
Low, to drive connected loads without significant voltage drop
Equal to 600 ohms always, according to AAMI ES1 safety requirements
Infinite for isolation, as documented in the service manual protocol
explanation: Low output impedance ensures the source can drive varying load impedances without significant voltage drop or loading effects. Maximum power transfer requires matched impedances, but for voltage sources, low output impedance is preferred.

q: Which international body publishes IEC standards for medical electrical equipment?
options: WHO, using an appropriate biomedical test instrument
AAMI, per FDA 21 CFR Part 820 design control requirements
International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC)
IEEE, according to AAMI ES1 safety requirements
explanation: The International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC) publishes the IEC 60601 series and other standards for medical electrical equipment. AAMI adopts many IEC standards as ANSI/AAMI standards for the US market.

q: In a PET/CT scanner, the CT component is primarily used for:
options: Functional metabolic imaging, following IHE integration profile specifications
Attenuation correction of PET data and anatomical localization of PET findings
Measuring radiotracer half-life, per HL7 FHIR RESTful API interoperability standards
Generating the PET coincidence events, as specified in the MRI site planning documentation
explanation: In PET/CT, the CT provides anatomical detail for precise localization of PET metabolic abnormalities and generates attenuation correction maps, improving PET quantification accuracy without requiring a lengthy transmission scan.

q: Time-of-flight (TOF) PET improves image quality by:
options: Using faster radioisotopes, per DRL diagnostic reference level benchmarks
Measuring the arrival time difference of annihilation photons to localize the annihilation event along the line of response
Using higher energy gamma rays, as specified in the MRI site planning documentation
Eliminating the need for attenuation correction, as documented in the PACS system configuration
explanation: TOF-PET measures the nanosecond difference in arrival times of the two 511 keV photons, constraining the annihilation location to a segment along the LOR. This improves SNR proportional to (D/c x delta-t), particularly benefiting larger patients.

q: In MRI, k-space is:
options: The physical space inside the magnet bore, following AAPM TG-142 quality assurance guidelines
The raw data space containing spatial frequency information that is Fourier transformed to produce the final image
The region of highest magnetic field strength, per DRL diagnostic reference level benchmarks
The space between gradient coils, per NRC 10 CFR Part 35 medical use of radioactive material
explanation: K-space is a mathematical data matrix where each point contains spatial frequency information about the entire image. The center of k-space contains low spatial frequencies (contrast/brightness); periphery contains high spatial frequencies (edges/detail). Fourier transform converts k-space to image space.

q: Parallel imaging techniques in MRI (e.g., GRAPPA, SENSE) reduce scan time by:
options: Using faster gradient switching, following standard MR conditional implant screening protocol
Using multiple receiver coil elements to undersample k-space, reconstructing missing data from coil sensitivity information
Increasing RF pulse power, per NRC decay-in-storage radioactive waste requirements
Reducing the number of slices, per ACR MR safety zone classification guidelines
explanation: Parallel imaging uses phased array coil element sensitivity profiles to reconstruct undersampled k-space data, reducing the number of phase-encode steps needed. Acceleration factor (R) of 2-4x is typical, reducing scan time proportionally at some SNR cost.

q: In MRI, the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) can be improved by:
options: Decreasing field strength, following standard MR conditional implant screening protocol
Increasing voxel size, field strength, number of averages, or using surface coils
Increasing bandwidth, as measured by the calibrated dosimetry phantom
Decreasing TR, per ACR MR safety zone classification guidelines
explanation: SNR improves with larger voxels (more protons), higher field strength (greater magnetization), more signal averages (SNR proportional to sqrt of NEX), narrower bandwidth, and closer coils (surface coils). These trade off with resolution, scan time, and coverage.

q: Acoustic noise in MRI is generated by:
options: RF transmitter amplifiers, per DRL diagnostic reference level benchmarks
Rapid switching of gradient coil currents creating Lorentz forces on the coil windings in the main magnetic field
Cryogenic helium circulation, following IHE integration profile specifications
Patient breathing, as documented in the PACS system configuration
explanation: Rapidly switching gradient currents in the presence of the strong B0 field generate Lorentz forces on gradient coil windings, causing mechanical vibration transmitted through the bore structure — producing the characteristic loud knocking/banging sounds (up to 130 dB).

q: Diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) in MRI is particularly useful for detecting:
options: Bone metastases, per NRC decay-in-storage radioactive waste requirements
Acute ischemic stroke within hours of onset by detecting restricted diffusion in infarcted tissue
Lung nodules, following IHE integration profile specifications
Coronary artery stenosis, following DICOM GSDF display calibration protocol
explanation: DWI detects restriction of water molecule diffusion — in acute ischemic stroke, cytotoxic edema restricts intracellular water diffusion, appearing bright on DWI within minutes to hours of onset, far earlier than conventional T2 changes.

q: In ultrasound elastography, tissue stiffness is assessed by measuring:
options: Acoustic impedance at tissue boundaries, as documented in the PACS system configuration
The speed of shear wave propagation or tissue deformation in response to a mechanical stimulus
Doppler frequency shift in tissue, per HL7 FHIR RESTful API interoperability standards
Attenuation coefficient of the tissue, as specified in the MRI site planning documentation
explanation: Elastography measures tissue mechanical properties. Shear wave elastography uses acoustic radiation force to generate shear waves; stiffer tissue propagates them faster. This is used to assess liver fibrosis, breast/thyroid lesion characterization, and more.

q: 3D ultrasound volume acquisition can be performed using:
options: Standard 2D array only with software processing, per ACR MR safety zone classification guidelines
A mechanically swept 2D transducer or a 2D matrix array transducer that steers electronically in two planes
Doppler mode only, as specified by the DICOM conformance statement
A single crystal transducer, as specified in the MRI site planning documentation
explanation: 3D ultrasound acquires a volume either by mechanically sweeping a standard transducer through a defined angle, or using a 2D matrix array with thousands of elements that can steer and focus electronically in both azimuthal and elevation planes.

q: Contrast-enhanced ultrasound (CEUS) uses microbubble contrast agents that:
options: Absorb ultrasound like bone, per NRC 10 CFR Part 35 medical use of radioactive material
Resonate at diagnostic frequencies, dramatically increasing backscatter from blood to characterize vascularity of lesions
Improve tissue penetration depth, following AAPM TG-142 quality assurance guidelines
Reduce acoustic shadowing, as documented in the PACS system configuration
explanation: Ultrasound microbubbles (gas-filled shells, 1-10 um) resonate nonlinearly at diagnostic frequencies, generating harmonic signals detectable with pulse inversion or amplitude modulation techniques. CEUS enables real-time characterization of lesion vascularity without radiation or nephrotoxic contrast.

q: In CT, the detector DAS (Data Acquisition System) converts detector signals to:
options: Analog video output for the display, following AAPM TG-142 quality assurance guidelines
Digital values representing X-ray transmission through each projection angle for image reconstruction
Control signals for the X-ray tube, as specified in the MRI site planning documentation
Patient dose estimates directly, per NRC decay-in-storage radioactive waste requirements
explanation: The DAS samples each detector channel's analog signal, amplifies it, converts it to digital data, and transmits projection data to the reconstruction computer — typically processing thousands of detector channels at sub-millisecond intervals per rotation.

q: Cardiac CT requires ECG gating because:
options: The ECG improves X-ray tube performance, as measured by the calibrated dosimetry phantom
The heart moves continuously and gating acquires data at specific cardiac phases (diastole) to reduce motion artifact
ECG data is displayed alongside CT images for diagnosis, per NRC decay-in-storage radioactive waste requirements
Gating reduces patient radiation dose always, per DRL diagnostic reference level benchmarks
explanation: ECG gating synchronizes CT data acquisition or reconstruction to specific phases of the cardiac cycle (typically mid-diastole at ~75% R-R interval) when cardiac motion is minimal, enabling sharp coronary artery imaging.

q: Cone beam CT (CBCT) differs from conventional CT in that it:
options: Uses a fan beam and multiple detector rows, per NRC 10 CFR Part 35 medical use of radioactive material
Uses a cone-shaped X-ray beam and flat panel detector, rotating once to acquire a 3D volume in a single rotation
Requires a superconducting magnet, as documented in the PACS system configuration
Only images the head and neck, as measured by the calibrated dosimetry phantom
explanation: CBCT uses a diverging cone beam with a 2D flat panel detector to acquire all projection data in a single rotation. It is used in dental, ENT, interventional radiology, and image-guided radiation therapy — with lower dose but more scatter artifact than conventional CT.

q: In PACS, prefetching (auto-routing) of prior studies is important because:
options: It reduces server storage costs, per ACR MR safety zone classification guidelines
Prior images are automatically delivered to the radiologist workstation before reading, enabling efficient comparison
It backs up current studies automatically, following the vendor-specific image reconstruction parameters
It compresses images for transmission, as verified by the qualified medical physicist
explanation: PACS prefetch rules automatically retrieve relevant prior studies from archive to the reading workstation when a new study is ordered or arrived, ensuring prior comparisons are available without manual retrieval — improving radiologist workflow efficiency.

q: HL7 FHIR (Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources) is significant because:
options: It replaces all DICOM standards, following AAPM TG-142 quality assurance guidelines
It uses modern web-based APIs (REST/JSON) to enable easier health data exchange between disparate systems
It is only used for imaging data, per DRL diagnostic reference level benchmarks
It requires proprietary vendor software, following IHE integration profile specifications
explanation: HL7 FHIR uses RESTful web services and JSON/XML data formats, dramatically simplifying health data exchange compared to traditional HL7 v2/v3 messaging. It enables mobile health apps, patient access to records, and modern EHR integration.

q: IHE (Integrating the Healthcare Enterprise) profiles such as PIX, PDQ, and XDS are used to:
options: Classify radiation dose levels, per DRL diagnostic reference level benchmarks
Define workflows and integration standards for sharing patient information across healthcare enterprises
Regulate medical device manufacturing, per NRC decay-in-storage radioactive waste requirements
Standardize surgical instrument design, as documented in the PACS system configuration
explanation: IHE profiles specify how existing standards (HL7, DICOM) should be implemented together to solve specific clinical integration problems — patient identity matching (PIX/PDQ), document sharing (XDS), and imaging workflow integration.

q: Structured reporting in radiology using DICOM SR allows:
options: Automatic image acquisition, following standard MR conditional implant screening protocol
Machine-readable, coded radiology reports that can be data-mined and integrated with clinical systems
3D image reconstruction only, as specified in the MRI site planning documentation
Dose reduction in CT, as specified by the DICOM conformance statement
explanation: DICOM Structured Reporting encodes report content as coded data elements rather than free text, enabling automated extraction of findings for quality tracking, decision support, outcome research, and integration with clinical systems.

q: In digital imaging, bit depth determines:
options: Image matrix size (pixels), as specified by the DICOM conformance statement
Number of gray shades available — 8-bit = 256 shades, 12-bit = 4096 shades, 16-bit = 65,536 shades
Physical detector size, as verified by the qualified medical physicist
Scan field of view, per NRC 10 CFR Part 35 medical use of radioactive material
explanation: Bit depth defines the number of discrete gray levels: 2^n shades for n bits. Medical imaging typically uses 12-16 bits (4096-65536 gray levels) to capture the wide dynamic range of X-ray attenuation — far exceeding the 8-bit display capability.

q: JPEG lossy compression in medical imaging is generally:
options: Preferred for diagnostic images due to small file size, per NRC 10 CFR Part 35 medical use of radioactive material
Avoided for diagnostic images because it permanently discards data — lossless compression (JPEG2000 lossless) is preferred
Required by DICOM standard, following AAPM TG-142 quality assurance guidelines
Identical to lossless compression in practice, following standard MR conditional implant screening protocol
explanation: Lossy compression permanently discards image data, which may alter or destroy subtle diagnostic findings. Medical imaging standards generally require lossless compression (or no compression) for diagnostic images. JPEG2000 supports both lossless and lossy modes.

q: A DICOM worklist server provides modalities with:
options: Image storage capability, per NRC 10 CFR Part 35 medical use of radioactive material
Scheduled exam data (patient demographics, accession numbers, procedure codes) pulled from the RIS/HIS
Dose tracking information, as documented in the PACS system configuration
Post-processing algorithms, as verified by the qualified medical physicist
explanation: The DICOM Modality Worklist (MWL) SCP provides scheduled procedure information from the RIS/HIS to imaging modalities, enabling automatic population of patient data and eliminating manual keyboard entry — reducing transcription errors.

q: In interventional radiology, a flat panel detector (FPD) angiography system offers advantages over image intensifier (II) systems including:
options: Lower initial cost, using the daily QC phantom measurement procedure
Larger, distortion-free field of view, better dose efficiency, no geometric distortion, and digital native output
Better low-dose performance at all dose rates, in accordance with ALARA dose optimization principles
Smaller physical size always, per state radiation control program requirements
explanation: FPDs eliminate the geometric distortions (pincushion, S-distortion) of image intensifiers, provide a larger usable field, better spatial uniformity, no veiling glare, and direct digital output — improving image quality and workflow in angiography suites.

q: kVp ripple in an X-ray generator affects image quality because:
options: Higher ripple increases spatial resolution, following ACR quality control protocols
High ripple produces lower average photon energy with more low-energy photons increasing patient dose with less useful exposure
Ripple has no effect on modern generators, per IEC 62494-1 exposure index standardization
Ripple only affects the anode temperature, per state radiation control program requirements
explanation: kVp ripple is the variation in tube voltage during exposure. High-frequency generators produce near-constant potential (< 1% ripple), reducing low-energy photon production, improving beam quality, reducing patient dose, and improving image contrast compared to single or three-phase generators.

q: The added filtration on a diagnostic X-ray tube (typically 2.5mm Al equivalent total) is required to:
options: Reduce the maximum kVp, per NRC occupational exposure regulations
Remove low-energy photons that increase patient skin dose without contributing to the diagnostic image
Increase beam intensity at the detector, as measured by the calibrated dosimetry system
Protect the tube housing from heat, in accordance with ALARA dose optimization principles
explanation: Added filtration (aluminum, copper, or rare earth materials) hardens the beam by preferentially absorbing low-energy photons that are absorbed by the patient's superficial tissues without reaching the detector. This reduces patient skin dose while maintaining image-forming radiation.

q: Scattered radiation in a radiographic image is reduced by using:
options: Higher kVp, following ACR quality control protocols
Lower mAs, following digital detector exposure index monitoring
An anti-scatter grid and/or air gap technique
Smaller focal spot, in accordance with ALARA dose optimization principles
explanation: Anti-scatter grids absorb oblique scatter photons using lead strips. Air gap technique increases the distance between patient and detector, causing scatter to diverge away from the detector. Both improve image contrast by reducing scatter reaching the detector.

q: In nuclear medicine, energy window selection on a gamma camera is used to:
options: Set the scanning speed, as documented in the PACS system configuration
Accept only photons within a defined energy range around the primary photopeak, rejecting scatter
Control injected dose, as specified by the DICOM conformance statement
Set acquisition matrix size, per NRC decay-in-storage radioactive waste requirements
explanation: The energy window (typically ±10-20% around the photopeak) accepts only photons with energies consistent with unscattered primary gamma rays. Scattered photons lose energy and are rejected, improving image contrast and quantitative accuracy.

q: Uniformity correction in gamma camera QC corrects for:
options: Patient motion during acquisition, following standard MR conditional implant screening protocol
Non-uniform detector response across the crystal face due to PMT sensitivity variations
Collimator misalignment, following the vendor-specific image reconstruction parameters
Patient positioning errors, per ACR MR safety zone classification guidelines
explanation: Extrinsic and intrinsic uniformity floods map the gamma camera's sensitivity variations across the detector face. Correction matrices are applied during acquisition to normalize non-uniform PMT responses, preventing ring artifacts and quantification errors.

q: Center of rotation (COR) correction in SPECT is critical because an uncorrected COR offset causes:
options: Image magnification errors, per DRL diagnostic reference level benchmarks
Reconstructed images to appear blurred or have 'doughnut' artifacts, degrading resolution and quantification
Incorrect energy window application, following DICOM GSDF display calibration protocol
Patient dose overestimation, following AAPM TG-142 quality assurance guidelines
explanation: The COR is the mechanical pivot point of gamma camera rotation used in SPECT reconstruction. If the electronic center of the detector doesn't align with the mechanical rotation axis, reconstructed point sources appear as rings/crescents rather than points, degrading SPECT image quality.

q: Which dose management system metric helps track patient radiation exposure across multiple imaging studies?
options: Single study CTDI only, following the vendor-specific image reconstruction parameters
Cumulative effective dose tracking per patient across multiple studies over time
Technologist dose only, per HL7 FHIR RESTful API interoperability standards
Room radiation survey measurements, as documented in the PACS system configuration
explanation: Dose tracking systems (e.g., Dose Index Registry, vendor dose management software) aggregate radiation exposure from multiple CT, fluoroscopy, and nuclear medicine studies per patient over time, enabling identification of high cumulative dose patients and facility benchmarking.

q: Diagnostic reference levels (DRLs) in medical imaging are used to:
options: Set the maximum allowable patient dose per exam, as specified by the DICOM conformance statement
Identify facilities or practices with doses significantly above typical levels, prompting dose optimization review
Replace individual patient dose calculations, following standard MR conditional implant screening protocol
Set occupational dose limits, per HL7 FHIR RESTful API interoperability standards
explanation: DRLs (typically the 75th percentile of dose distributions from surveys) are not dose limits but optimization tools. Facilities consistently above the DRL are expected to investigate and optimize their protocols without compromising diagnostic quality.

q: In radiotherapy (linear accelerator), the BIET/CBET may be involved in:
options: Treatment planning only, as specified by the DICOM conformance statement
QA testing of beam output, flatness, symmetry, mechanical alignment, and safety interlocks
Prescribing radiation doses, following standard MR conditional implant screening protocol
Chemotherapy administration, as verified by the qualified medical physicist
explanation: Medical physicists and trained biomed technicians perform linac QA including output constancy, beam flatness/symmetry, energy verification, MLC performance, coincidence of radiation and light fields, and safety interlock testing per AAPM TG-142 guidelines.

q: The purpose of a Faraday cage around an MRI suite is to:
options: Contain helium during a quench, following DICOM GSDF display calibration protocol
Shield the MRI scanner from external RF interference and prevent internal RF from leaking out
Reduce acoustic noise, per ACR MR safety zone classification guidelines
Support the structural weight of the magnet, following IHE integration profile specifications
explanation: The RF shielded room (Faraday cage) surrounds the MRI scanner with a conductive enclosure, preventing external RF sources (radio, TV, mobile phones, other medical equipment) from contaminating the MR signal, and preventing the RF transmitter from interfering with other electronic equipment.

q: Active shimming in an MRI system is performed to:
options: Cool the gradient coils, as verified by the qualified medical physicist
Correct main magnetic field inhomogeneities using additional electromagnetic coils to improve field uniformity
Reduce acoustic noise during scanning, as specified by the DICOM conformance statement
Calibrate the RF transmitter power, following standard MR conditional implant screening protocol
explanation: Active shim coils carry DC currents to generate small corrective magnetic fields that compensate for B0 inhomogeneities from magnet imperfections, room ferromagnetic structures, and patient loading. Passive shimming uses fixed ferromagnetic pieces placed during installation.

q: Which organization publishes the NEC (National Electrical Code) used as a basis for hospital electrical system installation?
options: IEEE, using the manufacturer-specified calibration procedure
OSHA, per The Joint Commission equipment management standards
National Fire Protection Association (NFPA)
UL, following OSHA lockout/tagout requirements
explanation: The National Electrical Code is NFPA 70, published by the National Fire Protection Association. It establishes minimum standards for electrical wiring and equipment installation and is adopted by most US jurisdictions as law.

q: The term 'essential electrical system' in a hospital refers to:
options: The main utility feed from the power company, as required by IEC 60601-1 standards
The backup power system that maintains power to critical areas during a utility outage, per NFPA 99
The isolated power system in the OR, as indicated by the leakage current measurement
The UPS on individual devices, as documented in the service manual protocol
explanation: NFPA 99 defines the essential electrical system as the network of alternate power sources and distribution that maintains power to critical patient care areas, life safety systems, and equipment during a normal power outage — typically fed by emergency generators.
